225297805.review:
[h2] The City Must Survive, and My VRAM is Leading the Charge [/h2]

After nearly 100 hours of staring into the frozen abyss, I’ve realized that Frostpunk isn't just a city-builder, it’s a high-stakes Resource Management & Maintenance Simulator. As an aircraft technician, keeping the Generator humming at -150°C feels exactly like a critical engine troubleshoot, zero room for error.
[olist]
    [*]Visuals & Performance
Running this on the RX 9060 XT is a treat. The heat distortion from the steam vents and the way the frost creeps across the screen is breathtaking. It’s the only game that makes me feel cold even when my PC is pumping out enough heat to warm my room.
    [*]The "Iron Man" Mentality
Getting the "Iron Man" achievement (The Arks) is my proudest moment. It requires the kind of precision where a single minute of downtime or a mismanaged coal pile leads to total system failure. No active pause in Survivor mode? That's just real-time troubleshooting under pressure.
    [*]Ethics vs. Efficiency 
Most games ask if you want to be "Good" or "Evil." Frostpunk asks if you want to be "Efficient" or "Dead." I’ve signed laws that would make a dictator blush, but hey at least the Generator is still humming and the soup is (mostly) warm.
    [*]The Verdict
If you enjoy optimizing heat maps, calculating coal-to-steam ratios, and losing sleep over a fictional city’s survival, this is the gold standard. It’s brutal, beautiful, and highly addictive.
[/olist]
[i] [b] 10/10. 24-hour shifts are mandatory, but the Moonshine is on the house. [/b] [/i]

I love Frostpunk. I've come back to playing it a couple of times and it never disappoints. It's like a comfy blanket you can snuggle into when you're sick or something -- which is how I was when I got the Ultramarathon Extreme achievement.

The music is great, the story is great, the atmosphere is incredible. The mechanics are pretty much what you'd expect from a city management sort of game and it's pretty easy to pick up. 

My only gripe really, is that the end game cutscene where it gives you a Fallout-style summary of your actions is a bit heavyhanded with browbeating you over some options, and it loves to give you the bad ending text for relatively mild transgressions, like breaking up riots or whatever.

224269313.review:
Still a fun game.
Played it on iirc epic before where i had a free version but ofc. no DLCs were included.
Now i finally picked it up on steam but this time including all the DLCs.

It looks ok, sounds ok, plays ok.

Its simply a nice combination of well known and popular elements put together to a solid game.
A storyline about the world going down the drain due to a sudden ice age so to speak.
Which means youll have to make tough decisions bc people are dying one way or another if you dont.
Especially in higher difficulty and without careful planning and timing not everyone will survive.
You can basically play free mode, or adjust a story mission from let me build my city under a bit of pressure up to a rock paper scissors sort of gameplay where every decision you make at any hour of the day can possibly make or break your game.
Theres something for pretty much everyone to enjoy.
You have a city builder / resource management, and need to keep your city warm and people well fed.
Survival except not entirely like most of the usual games with a "survival" tag.
More management, less cooking steaks.
Theres active and passive sources of income.
You can scout the area surrounding your city to gather resources and secure some of those additional sources of income.
Build outposts and set up trade routes and such.
Research new tech for new/improved buildings and other upgrades.
Make laws depending on your playstyle, the specific situation, you favored outcome and so on.
I think the law for child labor is the most popular running gag in this game.
Do you send them to work or not? And to what degree and where.
Maybe after wandering through the snow for weeks and finally arriving on your destination,
theres nearly as many kids as there are workers.
And those alone wont be able to provide what everyone needs just to make it through the 1st days.
Thats what i meant with the tough decisions.
Youll need a whole different "non civilized days" sort of a mindset.^^
Maybe send em to shelters so they dont get funny ideas turning into rowdys smearing grafittis on walls.
Could make em do easy jobs, let em work in the medical or science sector for small boots to those.
Plenty of ways to deal with things, and depending on the scenario, diffuculty etc. the "best" approach may vary.
Or you simply play a nice or evil run in order to grab some challenges o rmake your own.

And for me always the toughest decision is wether i let every refugee into my city or not.
Simply bc it could be already too full.
Theres simply no more room to expand, resources wont last a week, and you can already smell the next riot that some political and or religious queers are planning.

EDIT: You can keep those groups happy too by making and keeping "promises" such as building and or maintaining something.
They can also be played against each others in order to lets say weaken the biggest party, the one fraction you dont like, or simply get rid of the main troublemakers.

You have a system that reflects peoples mood.
If they freeze, are hungry, or havee other issues they will lose hope, and discontent will rise.
You might end up with seperatist movements or just fractions fighting each others about the better solution to some issues.
Propaganda might keep em happy, guards might keep troublemakers in check and secure neighbourhoods.

Lots going on and a lot of choices.
Which means replay value to try a different approach,

Might update as i keep playing if anything else comes to my mind.
Been a while since i was really familiar with the details of the game.

224200426.review:
Frostpunk is a city-builder similar to Cities: Skylines, but set in a frozen apocalypse where temperatures drop below -120°C. The world is dying under endless frost, and your only goal is to organize your society well enough to survive.
You'll manage coal, wood, and metal while sending scouts to search for new survivors.  But be careful because every new worker is also another mouth to feed.
The game will force you into brutal decisions: Do you send children to work in the factories, or do you build a nursery? Do you give your dead a proper burial, or do you use their remains as fertilizer for the greenhouse? Every resource counts. Frostpunk doesn't just challenge your strategy skills, it challenges your morality.

223885022.review:
Overall grade: B++

Enjoyable? Yes, and addictive
Recommended? To anyone interested in rss management, city builder type games and post-apocalyptic themes
Replayability: Med (I completed every scenario twice—and not for achievements)

+high-stakes, apocalyptic, survival/city builder
+gameplay: manage hunger, temperature, health and work assignments. Gather resources, build shelter and medical posts, research new technologies, enact laws (and more)—all while keeping your people’s hope up and discontent low
+presentation: beautiful, grim art and atmosphere
+OST: really, check it out. Most in-game sounds were good too

+/-this is NOT a relaxing city builder. It’s actually pretty brutal and unforgiving, intentionally so
+/-learning curve: expect to fail each scenario multiple times 

-philosophical themes presented throughout are only skimmed upon

Very minor gripes:
-difficult to tell structures apart—they all look very similar, especially when covered in snow, which can sometimes be a pain

Steam Deck compatibility: ⚠️ 
Tiny font size (~2 point font?) It is sometimes impossible to read but almost always extremely difficult—definitely strained my eyes
Runs smoothly otherwise but is surprisingly taxing on the hardware: <2hrs at full battery with max graphic settings @60fps. I personally lowered in-game graphical settings, tinkered with the steam deck settings, and got ~4hs @30fps
Widescreen ✔️

223863310.review:
if i could change my thumbs down to a pair of bootycheeks, i would. coz this game is STRAIGHT cheeks.

EDIT: TLDR; as many other reviews say, this game gives u the illusion of choice. theres about 100 ways u can choose to play- and inevitably lose. then theres the way the devs WANT u to play so u can win. none of the decisions really significantly affect anything and the game is incredibly linear and tedious when u realise u arent allowed to play the way u want. the people that tell u that this is a skill issue are the ones that wasted their lives decrypting the devs mystifing approach to game design: thru trial and error and many MANY wasted hours figuring out how the devs wanted them to play their game.

Ive tried very VERY hard to like this game. I dont want to trash it because clearly a lot of love and effort went into making it. i love evthing except the actual "gameplay". it should be cool. But its about as much fun as setting off a rat trap w ur scr*tum.

its a game about decision making that punishes every decision u make. i got as far as the winterholme event just by the skin of my teeth, only for my hope meter to drop thru the floor for no reason. the whole bleak grim-dark thing feels really forced and corny because of stuff like that. u never have enough men/resources and u have to spread them out over like 6-7 dif things. even w evone gathering coal and hydro monitors up u still run thru it faster than u can gather it, and thats only heating the hospitals w a steam hub. whatever resource ur short on will be the one the game suddenly decides u need. im sure theres a strategy guide on how to play, but if theres only one way to win why pretend like the player has any agency? if evone has to play the same way/follow a guide why are there different decisions? id much rather figure it out on my own over the game smacking my hand away and wagging its finger no at me.

its one of those games that has that infuriating anachronism where the worse ur doing the harder the game becomes. like bioshock infinte where u lose money evtime u die. why not dynamic difficulty like l4d's ai director? if u have to restart the game like 60+ times because u run into a dead end, maybe the game just sucks? im sure there are turbovirgins who would insist that the game is flawless and im just stupid (theyre wrong; the game def is not flawless). the type that think having to micromanage people in a city builder is the best thing ever, but i actually like to have fun from time to time. ive tried just about every strategy and nothing seems to work. when something does, it feels like chance. the game teaches u how to play by u wasting time losing a tonne and starting over. its not the 90s anymore, im not asking for yellow paint evwhere but its ok to let the player know if/how to play the game correctly. sinking a couple hours into a save only to find out ur in a culdesac is VERY epic.

i grew up playing fallout 1 and 2 and those were SIGNIFICANTLY less punishing and abstract than this game, while having much better atmosphere. this game just feels like a chore to play. maybe im just too stupid to understand the game, but i like to do work when im at work. not when im at home. the slightest mistake is almost exclusively met w a borderline save ending catastrophe. constantly being on the brink of losing is the opposite of fun. it feels like trying to stop a semitrailer from tipping over w ur bare hands.

"well the games not supposed to be fun" bruh thats like saying the food u sell at ur restaurant isnt supposed to taste good. if i wanted to torture myself id go out the back w some crocodile clips and a car battery. at least that wouldnt cost like 40 dollars

223691678.review:
A grim, high-stakes survival experience where you lead the last city on Earth. It’s a game about making tough choices in a world that wants to freeze you to death.

The core of the game is managing the "Generator," the massive coal-fired heart of your city. Everything revolves around heat. If the temperature drops and your coal runs out, your citizens freeze, fall ill, and eventually revolt. 
It is both stressful and incredibly rewarding when you manage to stabilize your supplies.

What sets Frostpunk apart is the Book of Laws. You aren't just managing coal, you are managing society. To survive, will you sign a law to put children to work in the mines? Will you stretch food supplies by mixing in sawdust? The game forces you into uncomfortable positions. 

The presentation and graphics look great and fit the theme.

Also the game has incredible sound design and a unique soundtrack that greatly enhances the experience.

If you enjoy games that challenge your ethics as much as your tactical skills, this is a must-play. Just don't expect to come out of it feeling like a hero.

[h1] 10/10 [/h1]
[h2] [i] Stress Management Simulator: Endless Winter Edition [/i] [/h2]

A truly hardcore survival experience. Frostpunk brings unique aspects to the city-builder genre that make micromanaging your colony incredibly rewarding... as long as things go your way.

If it's your first time playing, you will fail your first run. And that's exactly how it should be, it's part of the fun. The learning curve makes finally beating the main scenario feel absolutely incredible. The sense of relief and fulfillment you get from saving your people is unmatched.

Highly recommended! The additional scenarios are brilliant too, mixing things up with totally different environments and survival mechanics.

Got this and all DLCs for something like $5 - haven't touched anything besides the main story at the time of review. The main story is relatively short but it'll get many attempts to get there. Takes about 10h to figure out how everything works and get through the chapter. 

The gameplay is extremely stressful - you need to constantly pay attention to basically everything and, towards the end, when every possible alarm is beeping and everyone is freezing to death, it feels like you are on a very scary roller coaster... Yeah, neither I expected that from a city management game, yet here I am spending so many [i] consecutive [/i] hours to beat this...

I'd give this a 9.5 however, only because to understand how everything works - like the correct ratio of coal thumpers over gather posts - you'd most likely have to google it (the information might have been somewhere I didn't look, but that's also to be expected, when every tutorial is basically a few paragraphs on a menu on your top left). After you get the hang over it it's a solid 10 - for the money I spent.

223505615.review: Winning in Frostpunk feels like conquering the impossible. This game is a masterclass in blending survival mechanics with moral dilemmas, and every decision carries weight. The golden achievement I earned wasn’t just a badge it was proof of the countless tough calls, the sleepless nights managing resources, and the relentless drive to keep my people alive in a frozen wasteland.What makes Frostpunk so fun is its perfect balance of challenge and reward. The tension of rationing food, maintaining hope, and pushing technology forward keeps you on edge, but when you succeed, the payoff is immense. The atmosphere is haunting yet beautiful, with the frozen city glowing against the endless blizzard—a visual reminder of humanity’s resilience.The game doesn’t just test your strategy; it tests your morality. Do you enforce harsh laws to ensure survival, or do you preserve compassion at the risk of collapse? That constant push and pull makes every playthrough unique and deeply engaging.In short, Frostpunk is more than a city-builder it’s an emotional journey. Winning feels like a personal victory against nature itself, and the golden achievement is a shining testament to the fun, intensity, and brilliance of this game.

223505532.review:
[h1]Feels Too Restrictive[/h1]

Although I only played for about an hour, it was already clear that resources are very limited. You’re forced to build certain structures just to keep storage balanced across different resource types — otherwise, you won’t last long.

This creates a “hidden” build order the game expects you to follow. Even if it tries to disguise it, the freedom isn’t really there. And that kind of design kills the fun for me.

Consider carefully before buying — it might not be as enjoyable as you expect.

223354012.review:
I bought this one sale because it looked cool. I went in completely blind to what the experience was going to be. I was looking for a unique city builder game, which to be fair to this game is what it is. Quickly I realized however, that this game wasn't for me. 

First off there is no tutorial. You basically have to click some circle in the upper left to read the most vague description of how things work. Tool tips don't all appear at once, but rather as you play the game. So by the time you read something that could have been useful, you already messed up the run you were on. 

The game is unforgivingly difficult, not just from a micromanaging aspect, but from dealing with the discontent and hope bars which you have to maintain proper balance with or else the run is over. You aren't just fighting the conditions, but your own people's moods. Unfortunately, the penalties for any action are too high. 

The resources are for most maps pretty scarce. You can build permanent mines and stuff to collect permanent resources, but they require a currency you can only find when scouting the world map. The currency is steam cores. 

You don't really get enough people to run anything efficiently and you constantly are battling hunger and sickness which ends up making people die and tipping the scales of discontent. It doesn't feel like there are any winning conditions, only losing ones. 

I haven't really be able to gain ground in any game mode or map. This game really isn't something that is for everyone. I'd argue it's really only for hardcore RTS players and strategy game fans who know how to play a game like this from the get go.

The idea, the atmosphere, and the design is cool, but it's just not for me.

There is no other game I can think of that activates my empathy to this degree. Sure, you can play as a ruthless autocrat but I have shed tears over this game while desperately clawing to save as many people as I possibly can, and making as many sacrifices as I must to do so. This game perfectly captures the feeling of the indomitable human spirit and will to survive no matter what the cost. The lives of my citizens and the people I am trying to save feel like they have real weight to me. This is the game that convinced me that I like city builders, and every scenario is a strong contender for best plot in the game. The DLCs all add interesting new mechanics and conflicts and feel worth the price, especially if there's a sale. I would recommend this game to anyone.
ABOVE ALL ELSE THE CITY MUST SURVIVE.

223231821.review:
Save and save often
This is not a city builder game. You need to survive your decisions. So before you write a Law or send a scout party out or build something or research something. SAVE......

As of writing I am about to start my 6th attempt. I think I know what I'm doing except I don't. I do know I need food, heat, houses, medical centres. I need research and materials.

Each game I played I got the balance wrong,, This time I will get it right.. Honestly I will..

Being serious now. This game gets inside your head. I gave up playing 3 times as I couldn't figure out why I kept loosing. My discontent level just erupted out of nowhere and I couldn't figure out how to stop it.
So I read a few tutorials and that didn't help much. I formulated a strategy that worked for a while then I lost again. I finally got to the Londoneers and massive temperature drops, you guessed it my discontent went through the roof as I couldn't heat my settlement fast enough, I went down the wrong technology tree and my Laws didn't work as I intended. So SAVE before you make a decision and go back and do it differently.

It all sounds negative but it isn't. That's the beauty of this game. You sometimes need to be cruel as much as you need to be kind to get through the challenges. There are lots to contend with, but the payoff is huge amounts of thinking, planning and juggling resources and getting that rush from knowing you will get through to tomorrow.

Spoiler.. Child labour is a must have. This is a Victorian timeline and no one gets a free ride.

I have no idea where today will lead to, I just know I can't stop playing

223051058.review:
As someone who is a really big fan of The Long Dark, snow and grim atmospheres, I was very much drawn to Frostpunk and I can't believe I haven't played it sooner. The way I'd describe it is City Skylines but if it was more survival focused while managing civilian needs that are necessary keep them unified (which in this case is discontent - how upset they are with you - and hope - how optimistic they are living under your rule).

Now I will warn you ahead of time, the game is also very challenging. I lost my first game playing this, but that's kind of the point. It's meant to really push and challenge you in such a way that doing the bare minimum is not enough, and if you want to succeed, you will need to go a mile with the very little resources the game gives you. But if you just want a relaxing city building experience, this game has that too so you don't haft to worry about too much all at once, which is what I've been having fun with and something I appreciate when games include.

Overall, very solid game. I'd give Frostpunk a 9/10 and would highly recommend anyone who likes city building or challenging experiences to try it out.

223037162.review:
TL:dr;
For me it is 6/10 because I am a fan of steampunk. If you are not a steampunk fun, this is going to be strange experince for you. 
Game is old by now, but despite this is very taxing on even very modern graphic cards, game mechanics holds promise for few first hours and then fizzles out, turning into a slog of few clicks and running game on high speed just to get to the next checkpoint.
Also, the game is NOT moddable.


Graphics: 6.5/10
- mix of decent graphics like overworld and terrain, with very ugly buildings that are hard to tell apart.
- visuals for story line are nice, character portraits are very mediocre.
Visually this game is mixed bag. Somehow there is so much smoke on the map that it obscures the buildings and it it very hard to tell which is which. 

Sound: 5/10
- absolutely atrocious music, I have turned it off because it was driving me crazy.
- +2 for nice story VO, but there are very few of them.
- +2 somewhat decent map ambience when zoomed out.
- aaaand big minus for other sounds. While ui sounds are acceptable, nothing else is memorable and stands apart, and in fact some buildings generate annoying noises, when you zoom in.

Storyline & lore: 7/10
So this part is what made Frostpunk franchise. This is what people loved, this is what they wanted more of.
Story line is decent, it takes place in Victorian times, and presents end of the world scenario where humanity is trying to survive against cold and ice apocalypse, by building number of steampunk heat generators.
This is a great premise and with decent vigniettes makes for a captivating story.
Unfortunatelly, it is very shallow and many things remain unexplored and unexplained. 
Imagine Arcane: Leage of Legends series, you love characters and setting, but you never learn what transpired between Sisko, Jinx and Vi, and while Hextech is cool, you have no idea where it came from.
So this would be Frostpunk lore level.

Game mechanics: 3/10
So while the premise of building a city around steampunk generator and surviving against harsh climate disaster sounds fantastic, how exactly it is done in game, is no so great.
- first, resources and resource gathering, is trully weird. Due to how rapid game progresses in early stages, you have no choice but to follow exact path that devs envisioned for you, otherwise your people start dying or rebelling. But if you do, you never have to worry about resources ever again!
- research is weirdly limited. For example there is "House insulation" reasearch, that helps house building to cope better with cold, but this does not apply to "Public House" building, which is kind of a pub. And there is no research for these public service buildings. Meaning that at some point in the game, every visitor to the pub will have a significant chance of getting sick. Game would benefit from more research for all the minor things.
- buildings themselves are visually ugly and make no sense. Why exactly should we build a house above the ground, rather than dig down for extra insulation? Why exactly there is no Large Mansion for multiple people, or multi-stage construction of Storm Shelter that would keep your people safe during the Great Storm Event? And why, pry tell, we are not building domes above and around the generator to keep all this heat in why letting the steam out? The greenhouse principle is ancient human knowledge, watching your city turn into favelas rather than massive victorian greenhouses just feels bad.
- some bugs are still present in the game, and more importantly, some achievements are not unlocking.
- speaking of achievements, tons of very dumb ones. Basically devs had a pitch meeting and decided to come up with a bunch of dumbest or most annoying achievements ever. These require you to follow a very precise route to unlock, and if RNG messes you up for some reason, you may as well restart. So instead of playing the game, you will be save scumming to get the outcome right. Let me give you an example: "Banksy - Deal with Londoners grafitti peacefully". So... what do you do? How do you do it? I never used violence agains them, but the achievement never unlocked. Or "Fisher King - Build the generator in The Last Autumn scenario without building foragers huts", yeah but these also act as scouts, so without them you miss lore and other resources required for building generator. So in practice you need to do an entire run, separatelly, just to get this one achievement.
This is not playing, it is achievement farming. Luckily for us now, there are guides and ChatGPT or Grok can give us a detailed step-by-step list for first 15 days.
But other than that, these achievements are bad and take away any fun from playing.
- and finally, expeditions and scouting. There could be so many things going on here on world map layer, instead expeditions are just click, wait, click again, wait, sometimes you find something, sometimes you need to have a risky choice(use resources or risk scout team). And thats it! Huge, wasted potential for a story driven narrative that pairs with expeditions, setting out safe houses for longer ones and so on. 

I could go on, but I'll stop here. I'll maybe mention that there is a bunch of DLCs for this game and a lot of the story is locked behind them. Literally, a prequel to main scenario and a sequel to main scenario is behind DLCs, wow.

Summary:
You only buy that game on sale, or if you are a great fan of steampunk (very few decent games in steampunk setting), this game is mediocre city builder with shallow story, annoying mechanics and a pinch of steampunk flavor.

222894175.review: This game is an absolute masterpiece! It captures a raw, gritty atmosphere that is incredibly immersive. Unlike the sequel, the scale here feels personal and rewarding because you actually build and manage your own city building by building. Every placement matters, and seeing your heated hub grow against the blizzard is far more satisfying than the abstract district system. If you want a survival builder where your choices feel immediate and impactful, this is the one to play!

222893090.review:
Overall a thumbs up. 

The Good:
Most Scenarios in the game avoid the most annoying forced crisis mechanics that games of this genre often suffer from, which is definitely a huge plus. All of the issues where mechanically in-line with the game-play, and it never felt like some sort of arbitrary wall or wholly forced settlement expansion was thrust upon me.  The city builder side is fine, there is nothing wrong with it, but it is not exactly deep. A perfectly competent "Crisis-City-Builder". 

The Neutral:
The "Main Campaign" is rather easy and short though, someone familiar with the "Crisis-City-Builder" Genre can easily beat the game in under five hours, and with only a dubious handling of the mechanics of the game.  The other 4 scenarios are also pretty straightforward, and can be beaten in a couple of hours each. 

The Bad:
The narrative/world intersection is mediocre, and there's only so many ways to describe "you explore a cold cave with some stuff in it". More pressingly, replay value is [i]extremely[/i] poor. This is not a game like Sim City or Civ, where you can constantly launch into new sandboxes and explore, which feels kind of disappointing for a "City Builder" game. The 5 scenarios are all very fixed and play in a linear fashion, and increasing the difficulty does not meaningfully change anything up. There [i]is[/i] an endless mode, but once you get a city up and running in pioneers once, you  quickly realize how little content there is to engage with. The city building side is just kind of shallow, and there's only so many RNG events to populate a map so exploration quickly devolves into and endless chain of duplicates and re-treads. 

Conclusion: A solid introduction for people new to the Genre who want an easier game to start out with. A short, but enjoyable, playthrough for veterans, but not worth full price in that context. I got the game of the year edition on sale for $5, and $5-$10 feels fair for the amount of gameplay I got out of it.

It's alright. Beautiful game, fun to play. 

But the developer's clear anti-communist centre-'left'-liberal social-democratic politics really shines through in the "refugee" scenario especially. Near universal in games and media from Germany or Poland, and I'm sick of it. TBF, media from other countries isn't that much better, but there's the occasional media product which isn't just bland soft left liberalism (sometimes with tokenistic pandering), cynical everyone-sucks-equally-so-don't-care-about-anything nihilism, or right-wing CIA reprogramming direct from Langley & the Pentagon (CoD).  Disco Elysium really was a one-off. 

Anyway, apart from that, this is one of the best city builders I've ever played, fantastic in terms of visual and audio and gameplay design. Buy it and give it a go, especially if it's on sale.

222795427.review:
TLDR: 8 out of 10 if you want a city builder that is also brutal and will kick your butt. Everyone else trying to build a city failed, you're going to find out why they failed and probably fail a few times as well. Eventually you will get it right but then the temptation to get "righter" will set in and cause another attempt. The game is bleak but also presents a strong message of hope, not giving up despite how bad it gets. The city must survive and it's your job to somehow make it happen.

Actual Review:
Value: So I bought this game on sale for like five dollars including all the DLC. With all the current content, I'd be willing to pay $40 at most. There is a lot of replay value. It is possible to finish the main story extremely quickly but only if you used a guide, cheated, or are somehow ungodly skilled at winter survival city builders.

Gameplay: So this is where a lot of people get thrown off. The main thing about Frostpunk is that it is BLEAK. There are some fantasy elements such as the automatons but it does take the cold realism quite seriously. This is a scenario that humanity would probably NOT survive. The game hammers that in very hard. I am starting off with this because this game purposely throws you into it with only a basic tutorial and you're expected to fail the first few attempts. Seriously, even if you save scum you might still be doomed. Every other city failed, you are the last city. You aren't the last city because you're the main character, you're the last city because you're probably going to die too until you've gotten good enough at the game.

Ok, difficulty warning out of the way. It's a city builder with a heavy focus on resource and population management. There aren't many resources, only: coal, steel, wood, raw food, cooked food, and your population. There are a few things such as prosthetics and steam cores which have special uses, though prosthetics is pretty self-explanatory. Steam cores are used in your fancy buildings and to make something neat that I won't spoil, but it makes your life a lot easier.

What makes it stand out is how it does not suggest a research path and the objectives are very simple. Get coal, get food, keep people happy, help others, don't die. The scenario of suddenly having to build a city from scratch with nothing but a coal-powered generator and eighty dudes is treated how it would be. Which means no advisor to hold your hand or tell you what you should do first. There are resources scattered around that you can grab manually or use a gathering post on, but how do you get more when the freebies are gone? Well it's pretty obvious if you take two seconds to look at the research tree and think about it, but it is still easy to get ambushed and realize you're running out of something and won't have the solution before it becomes a problem. You can also scout out the world and find things left behind and bring those back to the city, but you ACTUALLY have to bring them back. Scout party goes out, picks up all the stuff, and you can keep sending them to scout more but you don't get any of the stuff till they return.

Overall this means that the gameplay is less about adapting and more about planning ahead. Problems tend to kill you if you're unprepared. The game does give enough hints to figure out what is about to happen if you pay attention. If it tells you that a bunch of people are coming towards your city for shelter then you'd better be prepared to receive a bunch of people that just trekked through a frozen hell. You can even outright ignore nearly all the objectives except the "gather resources" one because you're going to do that unless you want your game to end in less than one in-game day. I won't spoil what happens if you choose to ignore the objectives; it's different each time.

So what's the gameplay? Survive. It does not matter how god-awful condition your city is in by the end, you just need to survive the winter of all winters. You're on a timer in a sense; certain things are going to happen whether you're ready for them or not. Though there is an endless mode if you want to just keep going. There are several scenarios where you try to survive again but each one throws a curveball at you. The game isn't super complicated, it doesn't have a ton of buildings or anything like that, but it is ALMOST impossible to research everything and it is impossible to see all the possible laws and choices in a single playthrough. So there is replay value beyond just the scenarios.

Performance: I had no issues, not even little ones that I can nitpick. But also I am using a very good PC for the time this game came out so I might not be the best metric.

Graphics: It has a very nice 2D art style for a lot of the events and cutscenes and the actual gameplay looks nice too. It's not super advanced but it is good for the time it came out and for what it is. You can see your citizens going to work, going home, and going about their day. It's not for show either; each person does have to physically go to their work site and home and wherever else they want to go. This actually is helpful for the gameplay because they can freeze during the commute and you want to avoid them walking through snow by building roads. The audio is nice too, best music I've seen in this genre, and the way the city announcer changes based on your laws and decisions is a nice touch. Also, the city building has a very nice "grid" to it so your city will end up looking very neat and organized... even if you're like me and keep messing up the placements and it's actually full of tiny gaps.

Music and Sound Design: Very good, couldn't find a single flaw in what was there. Only complaint is what wasn't there; I do wish there was more voice acting. You do hear your citizens and they do pop up as text bubbles but it's rare that anyone actually speaks to you.

222715835.review:
Frostpunk is one of the most intense and thought-provoking strategy games I’ve ever played. It’s not just about building a city — it’s about making impossible decisions and living with the consequences. Every choice feels heavy, whether you’re deciding between keeping people warm or enforcing strict laws to survive.

The atmosphere is incredible. The constant cold, the music, and the visual design really pull you into a world where survival is never guaranteed. It keeps you on edge the entire time.

What really sets Frostpunk apart is how it challenges your morals. It’s not just “win or lose” — it’s “what kind of leader did you become to survive?” That’s something most games don’t even try to explore.

If you like strategy games with depth, tough decisions, and a unique setting, Frostpunk is absolutely worth playing.

222621569.review:
Yet another in a long, long list of games that are popular for style over substance...

It's a...city-builder, political simulation, survival game, supposedly, but it actually is bad at all of those things.

The positives are solely the presentation. Like, sure, the music is very good at tugging on your heartstrings with those mournful violin chords...but that isn't actual substance.

The graphics are very good at presenting a frozen apocalypse. Same with the sound design. I shiver at seeing the snow drifts and hearing the frost sound cues...but that also isn't substance.

What do you do? You lead post-apoc survivors in...well not dying. Except...do you, really? I ask that, because a good game would give me options and allow flexibility, but not here.

I have a very narrow pool of initial population. Sure, I want to juggle and have to try to survive, it is a game after all, but it really feels like the resource values are arbitrarily tiny. In order to feed my starting people, reliably, I'd need about 50 of my 105 adults devoted to hunting and cooking. 

Now, bear in mind I also need people to harvest basic resources and man the hospitals and lab equivalents...I don't have much wiggle room and there is a ticking clock before the weather gets hellish...

Political sim? Wow, I can decide whether our children get to help with work or not or whether we can make soup or use sawdust to bulk out our food...bruh, laws can be changed and repealed and also those aren't mutually exclusive. Why can't I, the nominal leader, say, "sure, our kids can help in BOTH the labs and the medical clinics" or "you know what, who wants to eat, today? Let's buck up and make soup" without having everyone "lose hope." 

My people, it's arctic armageddon out there, let's focus on not freezing or starving before you all start contemplating mutual unalife pacts...

Is it a city-builder? Sure, there is a city and some building is required, but, like the people management you're just kind of wrong if you don't do certain things in certain ways along certain orders. Like, the game I just tried to play I didn't immediately ring the central heater with my tents. "Experts" are now cringing at my failure to understand the game, but what this means is that some of my housing was just outside the heat radius so they were slightly cold...

This then means that every night I had a "small chance" of my people getting sick which actually translates to every night 3-ish guys come down with the sniffles and can't work for a day or two because god forbid we just bundle up under the covers at night. Endless snowdrifts out there, but heaven help me if my adult human beings would wear a second coat...

It's limited...

It's beautiful, sure, but as a game it's...almost not actually a game. Some tryhard diehards are frothing at the mouth right now, but that's just honesty, man. If I have to follow a narrow build order and task sequence to win, then there is no strategy.

A Telltale game'd have more agency...at least there I can pick who lives or dies. Here, it's just meaningless "oh, you didn't build here? Tsk, tsk, guess you lost the game" yet it'll only tell me that like 3 hours in...

Not worth the hype and I'm sorry I ever spent money on this.

85% worth the money.
I don't know about other people, but this expansion was really fun to play. The complete contrast in gameplay from base frostpunk is welcomed.

This expansion gave me a solid 5-6 hours of enjoyment.

Pros:
- Loved the unique Laws and Research
- Enjoyed the constant time pressure to build the generator.
- Liked the worker safety mechanic, but felt it was a little simple.
- An amazing prequel to the main game.

Cons:
- Pricey for 1 scenario and an endless expansion.
- Foragers (Scouts in this expansion) felt lackluster.
- Apparently there's a scout bug (Never happened to me).
- Somewhat easy to complete (For me at least, required 1 replay after losing all motivation on day 12)
- No replayability whatsoever (Unless you want to pick a different law path)

In my honest opinion, definitely worth grabbing if you want some more content for frostpunk, however If you're looking for essentially a new frostpunk game/experience, then it's probably not worth the money.

222456771.review:
Genuinely great game, a complete package with at least 50 hours of experience baked into it (without any DLC).

For some reason, the community tab and playerbase is absolutely filled with Russians. I don't know why they would want to relive their everyday experiences for $30. Maybe dying in the cold, confined to a cramped homeless shelter, eating soup made of 80% sawdust, somehow speaks to the Russian soul.

There's only a couple critiques I have of this game. The real difficulty lies only in the first couple days (hours) of gameplay. Once you have solidified even a meager flow of resources, the threat of the cold slowly loses dissipates and the end game kind of falls flat. To be fair, most of the scenarios involve a huge, final blowout where your resources suddenly fail and you're forced to rely on whatever you've saved, but besides that test of frugality, there's very little compelling gameplay in those last couple days besides watching your little pawns frolic in the -158 degree blizzard or rapidly die off in their hovels.
Exploration into the wastelands is a very interesting idea, but unfortunately only involves waiting for a team to move, then waiting for them to move again. They can come back with big rewards, and sometimes there are risky events, but usually its just boring.
The game mechanics also annoyingly lack awareness of the player. Example: people get restless because their homes are too cold, so I agree to heat their homes. If I don't heat their homes, they will lose confidence in me and I could lose. As I turn on the heat, I decide to tinker around with which heat sources are the most optimal to keep on, so as not to waste coal. In this tinkering, I switch off a heater and immediately turn on a different one, but because I turned one off, I fail the challenge.
This is the kind of game that mods would bring up to a 10/10. I can think of a lot of small features and improvements to add.

222206709.review:
Frostpunk is a gripping city-building survival game that challenges not just your strategy, but your morality in the face of extreme conditions. It blends resource management with emotional decision-making, creating a tense and unforgettable experience.

🔹 Gameplay: At its core, Frostpunk is about survival under pressure. Managing heat, resources, and population becomes increasingly difficult as the cold intensifies. Every decision carries weight—whether it’s optimizing efficiency or making sacrifices for the greater good. The game constantly forces you to adapt, keeping tension high from start to finish.

🔹 World & Atmosphere: The frozen wasteland is both beautiful and oppressive. The towering generator at the center of your city serves as a constant reminder of survival, while the endless snow and storms create a sense of isolation and urgency. The atmosphere is one of the game’s strongest elements, pulling you deeply into its harsh world.

🔹 Storytelling: Rather than a traditional narrative, Frostpunk tells its story through your choices. The dilemmas you face shape the direction of your society, making each playthrough feel personal and impactful. It’s less about right or wrong, and more about what you’re willing to do to survive.

🔹 Presentation: The game features stunning visuals with detailed environments and a cold, muted color palette that enhances immersion. Combined with a haunting soundtrack, it delivers a powerful emotional tone that lingers even after you stop playing.

✅ Verdict: Frostpunk is more than just a city builder—it’s a test of leadership and ethics under extreme pressure. It stands out for its atmosphere, meaningful choices, and relentless challenge.

Score: 10/10 – A masterclass in survival strategy that leaves a lasting impression.

222174442.review:
[h2] 4 out of 5 Stars! [/h2]

This is a city-builder type of game where you build your oasis in a frozen world, survive brutal storms, manage your population, handle day-to-day work, keep track of resources, and grow a thriving city.
People can get sick, suffer frostbite, or go hungry, so you’re always on edge. Most scenarios take around ~40 days to complete cuz the final objective always happens around this time in-game.

There are several scenarios (DLC included) to play: the main story, two side stories, two prequels, and one sequel scenario.

It’s oddly addictive and definitely scratches my small OCD itch—I love building everything neatly and organized lol.
Also note, it’s challenging! This won’t be a walk in the park. You’ll probably lose a few times in each scenario while figuring out what works, unless you plan ahead and manage things smartly.

Overall, it's really fun to play.

221657850.review:
So after my first play-through where I sort of beat the first scenario I gave this a sort of ambivalent review 6.9 hours in because I thought - well, is that it?  I went back and played some of the other scenarios and then realised it was a different game to the one I thought it was.  So it definitely deserves a different review, which to be fair I promised once I looked at the other scenarios.

It's a well made, well presented, beautifully balanced game.  It's a game I originally took for a rather straightforward city-builder, but the truth is it's not that really, not at all.   It's actually a series of stories so my original comparisons with Rimworld are not crazy, in the sense that the game is constantly throwing things at you. Although I originally felt like I'd seen the whole game in one sitting actually that's not true at all.  Each scenario needs to be approached in a different way and forces you to explore different areas of the tech tree and that is actually extremely compelling and makes it highly replayable.  And once you get beyond the initial scenario you absolutely will not be breezing through the game in one play-through even on normal.

Comparisons?  My initial comparison to Tropico is fair (admittedly I only played Tropico 4) because of the short, scenario based gameplay.  I really like being able to complete a play-through in one sitting.  The game never lets you mess around, it's always forcing you into making genuine choices not least with the tech you focus on.  

It's not a city-builder, or a colony sim.  It's more of the genre of something like Utopia (I know that is going back some) or Age of Empires II where each scenario has it's own story.  Of course in Utopia you had to do exactly the same things every time, but this is really well designed how it presents the story and develops it.  It's a great challenge you can really immerse yourself in.  Today I played a scenario 3 times back to back until I beat it.  

I've lost my whole week to this game. I guess that means it's good.

220633992.review:
Surprisingly, I never left a review for this game. Frostpunk 1 is one of the finest (survival) city builders I have ever played. It combines simple yet in-depth building mechanics with an interesting setting. The Victorian Era steam-punk post apocalyptic winter hellscape is inspirational conceptually. There is just enough back story to establish the setting, but the bits of lore you pick up along the way and the events you encounter keep it interesting. Most of the scenarios, especially the primary one, are fairly easy to beat if you don't care about doing well, but can be extremely challenging if you are a perfectionist. The post game summary directly plays into this - making you wish you had done better when it reminds you how many people you left to die, calls you out your tyrannical decisions, chastises you for implementing child labor, or brings up that your leadership was so ineffective that half the population fled the city deciding that certain death in the frozen wilderness was preferable to your rule. The time elapse of your city being built during the post game summary is a really cool edition.

The biggest caveat is that most of the scenarios require knowing what you need in advance which you're only going to learn through trial and error (trying the mission multiple times).

DO NOT BE FOOLED! Frostpunk 2 plays almost completely differently than this. In my opinion, this is the better of the two and has continuously brought me back.

220572354.review:
[h1]Frostpunk[/h1]

[b]Frostpunk[/b] is the brutally cold city-builder survival game where you’re not just mayor—you’re basically the grim, frostbitten god of a last‑ditch human settlement. You build, you manage, you make impossible moral decisions, and you watch people freeze if you screw up. Think SimCity meets apocalyptic ice age with a heavy dose of “do I burn the sick kids to keep the rest alive?” vibes. The heart of Frostpunk is its blend of survival, resource management, and soul‑crushing moral choices that make you question if you’re a good leader—or just a really cold-hearted ice tyrant.

[b]What works well:[/b]
• Intense moral dilemmas: Every decision hurts someone, and that tension is chef’s kiss.
• Beautiful, bleak world: Frost, snowstorms, and frozen wastelands make for stunningly harsh scenery.
• Deep city-building & management: Infrastructure, heat, resources, and citizens’ hope all matter — neglect one and things go south fast.
• Engaging scenarios & survival mode: Multiple storylines and challenges keep you coming back for more.

[b]Some negatives:[/b]
• Stress levels high: Be prepared for existential dread—this game will emotionally wreck you.
• Micromanagement heavy: You will spend time clicking through citizen after citizen, which can feel tedious.
• Learning curve: Newcomers might get buried under systems and resource balancing.
• Some repetitiveness: Late-game can feel like constantly juggling firewood and hope meters.

[u]Overall:[/u]
Frostpunk isn’t just a game — it’s a test of leadership, morality, and patience. Every heatwave, snowstorm, and revolt makes you sweat (literally and figuratively) as you try to keep the last city alive. Perfect if you like cold strategy, tough choices, and heart-pounding city survival.

[b]Final verdict:[/b]
A brutal, icy masterpiece of strategy and morality — tense, thoughtful, and emotionally heavy. Frostpunk will make you feel like a god, a tyrant, and a therapist all at once, and it’s totally worth freezing your soul for.

Frostpunk is an interesting survival strategy builder, it has strong atmosphere, memorable story and some genuinely interesting decisions that make the first playthrough feel special. Replayability is limited though, many choices boil down to two options, and once you understand the systems, the mid/late-game loop gets repetitive. The scenarios are mixed some are excellent, but mostly play once, maybe twice unless you’re achievement-hunting.

Achievements are mixed, some are fun and clever, but a lot of them are grindy and not fun, also this is one o the games which just dumps all of the achievements no matter if you have DLCs which is always annoying. DLC value is also inconsistent, they feel overpriced for what they add to the base game. The one DLC that truly brings a distinct playstyle and fresh perspective is The Last Autumn, the rest feel more like extras without adding enough worth and in my opinion should be patches for the main game.

All in all it’s worth to play frostpunk for the unique campaign experience, but without long-term replayability once you’ve cracked the formula, as choices and playthrough style is limited and usually have a better/worse option instead of different playstyle options.

219534493.review:
I watched a let's play of this game back in 2018 when it first released, and it has been stuck in my mind ever since. 

I recently picked up the game along with all the DLC for super cheap during the Winter Sale (lol) and finally decided to give it a shot for myself, and I wasn't disappointed. I'm not usually the kind of person to play City Builder/RTS games, but something about the atmosphere and story of this game has always been very captivating to me. The game itself isn't especially difficult, at least on the lower difficulties, but it has a way of toying with your conscience and leaving a pit in your stomach when things go wrong which causes you to feel the need to pause the game and pace around the room, calculator in hand, to make quadruply sure that you have enough infirmary beds for the coming storm or enough resources for dreadnought shipments, and I love that about it. Everything feels so intimidating in such a way that it lets the dread truly seep in, and out of the love it's instilled for the setting I've spent many nights researching information on life at -60°C or looking up schematics for the generator to see if something like this would be possible in real life (spoiler: it kind of is). If you enjoy dark, story-based games with a tighter focus on micro-scale interactions you will very likely enjoy this game.

I would recommend getting both the base game alongside all the DLCs given that the DLCs provide another ~3x of playtime that A New Home provides for less than half the price of the base game. For reference, I currently have 84 hours on this game and I still haven't even started OTE or any of the Endless modes yet. However, it's worth noting that there are unfortunately still some bugs present, especially with the DLCs. They're not game-breaking by any means and can usually be sorted out by simply reassigning workers or saving and reloading to refresh the UI, but they do exist.

All in all, a very enjoyable experience and well worth the money. Just make sure to wait for a sale to buy it, because you can get everything for less than $8.

218604572.review:
A Chillingly Addictive Experience! Frostpunk is a game that - for me - stands out as a masterpiece of strategy and survival. At its core, it’s a challenging and deeply motivating experience—one that demands persistence, as the learning curve can feel steep at first. Once you grasp its mechanics, the sense of accomplishment is unparalleled. The game’s design is nothing short of brilliant. The mechanics are thoughtfully crafted, offering a balance of complexity and accessibility. Whether you’re rationing scarce resources, making gut-wrenching moral decisions, or tweaking the laws of your frozen society, every choice matters. 
The immersion is second to none, with the game’s atmosphere pulling you into its harsh, apocalyptic world. Visually, Frostpunk is still awesome in 2025. From the intricate details of the snow-covered city to the visual effects of the relentless cold, it’s clear that a lot of love and effort went into its presentation. The soundtrack deserves special mention—it's an absolute masterpiece. 

The additional DLCs elevate the experience even further. They’re incredibly creative, each offering fresh perspectives and new challenges. One particular level stands out as exceptionally interesting. 
If you are up for a challenge this game definitely tests your limits...!!!

A literal masterpiece of city-building simulation. I wish I had discovered this sooner. Well, more like I knew about it but never got around to playing it. I kept hearing people say the game was too challenging, and the aesthetic from still images didn’t look interesting enough until you actually got into it. The atmosphere is something you can feel immediately. The countless lives that depend on your leadership, the resource management, and the decisions and laws you have to make all combine into wonderful gameplay that is surprisingly straightforward and accessible.

The only hard part is whether you gathered enough resources for your colony before it is too late. The A New Home scenario really left me in awe. The music is incredible. It was a tiring battle until the end while getting through the Great Storm. Even if you fail, the game makes you want to keep trying, and you always come back to finally save humanity. Too bad I have heard Frostpunk 2 is not as great as this. But here is looking forward to the remaster, Frostpunk 1886.

Aight, this game for me, probably one of the best city-building out there, I really like the settings, when humanity trying to survive amidst the post-apocalyptic winter and dystopian-steampunk-esque world, like 99% percent of the earth surface are covered in snow and ice.

But, for me, what make this game interesting to me is how the society adapted in the extreme cold and unforgiving, never-ending snowstorm, how people there try to cling on something to believe in, or preventive drastic action, Faith and Order, the core of humanity themselves, what makes me intrigued the most.

Aside the gameplay mechanics truly fascinates me, for example, one of my favorite cases is I have to enact Child Labors law, because my settlement really undermanned, and I have to reload the save, because I really can't bring myself to do so. Again, drastic and difficult choices in order to survival, that raw human will at the edge of extreme measures, that's the reason I'm reeled to this game, makes you think and our society today are eerily closed to this somehow.

One of a kind, merciless and intimate City builder game imo, that's why this game deserved GOTY, and dunno I haven't tried the second entry, not really a fan of bigger scope, and more complicated world, might skipped it, and immediately jumped to Frostpunk 1886 next year. (8,8/10)

218360728.review:
I’ve played for about 5 hours so far, and I can confidently say this game delivers a unique and intense experience. The atmosphere is incredible — the frozen world feels harsh and immersive, and the soundtrack really adds to the emotional weight of your decisions.

What I enjoy most is the moral dilemma system. Every choice feels meaningful, and you’re constantly balancing survival with humanity. Managing resources, keeping people hopeful, and dealing with unexpected crises creates real tension that keeps you engaged.

That said, the learning curve can be a bit steep at first, and some mechanics aren’t immediately obvious. A little more guidance for new players would help. Also, early mistakes can snowball quickly, which might frustrate some players.

Overall, I would definitely recommend this game, especially if you enjoy strategy, city-building, and decision-driven gameplay with a darker tone. It’s challenging but very rewarding once you start understanding the systems.

218359878.review:
[b]Score: 10/10[/b]
[list]
	[*][b]Art:[/b] Excellent. The visual design conveys the cold so effectively that you can almost feel it through the screen. The city feels alive and constantly evolving, although it would be nice to have a slightly closer zoom level to better appreciate the NPCs and small details.
	[*][b]Music:[/b] Outstanding. Both the ambient tracks and the tense pieces during critical moments elevate the atmosphere and reinforce the emotional weight of decisions.
	[*][b]Story:[/b] Very strong. A survival story set during a glacial apocalypse within a steampunk society. The numerous events and different scenarios portray various stages of societal collapse and adaptation, making each settlement feel distinct and meaningful.
	[*][b]Gameplay:[/b] An original city builder where temperature management and keeping the population alive through a central generator are core mechanics. It also includes a secondary exploration layer with scouting teams, adding strategic depth. The wide variety of scenarios introduces new mechanics and twists that keep the experience fresh. Replayability is excellent thanks to multiple difficulty levels (including custom settings), and achievement challenges encourage creative playstyles such as zero-death runs or restrictions on specific systems.
	[*][b]Accessibility:[/b] Could be much better. Aside from difficulty modifiers, there are very few options. The game especially needs better font size customization, as text can be difficult to read even when sitting close to the screen.
	[*][b]Other:[/b] It may be worth waiting for the remaster, but even in its current form this version remains excellent.
[/list]

When most people think of a "cozy" game, they think Animal Crossing or that one lord of the rings hobbit game or like pokemon or something. Like soft management and just sorta relaxing. 
This is my cozy game. Which is maybe ironic. Or strange. But something about it is just so satisfying. And relaxing? It's reminiscent of old 2010 management sim games I used play on my old desktop computer in grade school, but just *better*. When you solve one issue, another one pops up. Fix the cold? Well now you have 50 more people wanting to join your city. Get them housed? Well now you need to feed them. Fed them? You're running low on coal, guy. Coal sorted? Oops, that heating issue you thought you solved wasn't enough and now half your city is about to die from sickness and your people are going to throw you out of the captain's chair. So then you decide that you're going to establish a dictatorship because they just don't understand how survival works. 
Then, when you get to the end of it all, you look at this complex network you've built, with everything running smoothly, and it just feels oh so right. Nevermind the war crimes you've commited. Try again next time, but maybe be the good guy.

217866987.review:
You ever thought that a bad guy would take over the world and rule with an iron fist?  This game is unique in that it removed that old trope and introduced a new type of villain, your environment.  This is arguably more realistic to endure than some bad guy.  The cold that comes with winter is unbearable and unending in Frostpunk.  The game explores what it would be like if 50 or so people band together to survive the elements.  

Along the way people can be discouraged, and lose hope.  As the leader your job is to make sure they're warm, fed, and happy.  Simple enough?  Well no. This game is impressive visually as well as deeply enriching in its lore.  Take the time to read all the pop ups.  Take the time to decide people's fates.

If you haven't had a chance to play and you have a newer PC there is a chance you might want to wait for Frostpunk 1886.  It is a sequel to Frostpunk that will capitalize on better visuals and new stories, as well as a remake of this game.  Frostpunk 2 is an entirely different game and not really suited as a direct sequel.

Word to wise, do not play this on a laptop. Play it on your desktop machine. See my updated comment at the end of this review.. 

Original Review 2/3/2026 - thumbs down.
I played 60hrs of this game. Managed to get through the main story and the additional scenarios... but performance was always an issue. The opening credits chugged along and sound stuttered. Opening scenario cutscenes performed just as poorly with garbage frame rates and sound stutter. I both recalibrated my laptop (per reddit and Steam support forums) and downgraded the in-game graphics settings and these areas just did not improve. The game play itself did perform fine but i never once got to watch these opening cutscenes. 
I failed the last scenario, The Edge, last night and came back today to try again and the total performance of the game has just tanked. Global performance is suddenly absolute garbage and unplayable. Nothing about my machine has changed in the 24 hours between play times. I uninstalled the game and tried again. nothin'.
This is extremely discouraging as i do not know what to do now with this 8 year old game. It seems this game has never gotten decent optimization from the devs that apparently have ditched this game for a lesser sequel by the judge of the steam reviews. Bummer for me.

Edit 2/4/2026 - Thumbs up
I installed the game on my PC and it's a night and day difference from my gaming laptop. Utterly different. Smooth like butter. Wild, because my laptop is newer and has comparable specs, but i know graphics cards on laptops are different than that of PC's - still. Anywho, it's a much more enjoyable experience. Changing my opinion to a thumbs up.

217372901.review:
The children yearn for the mines.

What more can I say that hasn't already been said? The game is fun, gorgeous to look at, with incredible music and riveting gameplay mechanics. It's one of those games that you can either play on easy as an idler type city builder or set it to normal and get your sh*t rocked. It's the kind of game I play for 10 hours+ one week, set it down for a month or so, and then hop back on. (And I still can't finish Winterhome damnit) The art style is incredible, the cinematics are insane and the gameplay is weirdly addictive. Coming from someone who never played Sim type city builders or any city builders in general, this was a pleasant surprise in terms of how captivating the gameplay is. Not only do you manage limited ressources but also literal human emotions like despair and hope, which can make it very tricky to get things done. Constant needs have to be met while battling the ever dropping temperature and it creates a tension like none I have ever experienced in a game. The boss music for the final storm gives me chills to this day, because I swear after spending a few hours managing these people, you feel like you're there watching the generator with them, praying, hoping that the storm will pass without too much illness and death. It is an incredible time. 

My biggest complaints are the lack of mod support and variety. After 100+ hours it starts to feel repetitive without new buildings or customization. Especially in endless mode I really wish there was more decoration options to really get into the "city-builder" side of it. You only get some very limited buildings to decorate with. Of course the main focus is more the survival aspect so I can understand the logic in foregoing those details. It's really great for the first few playthroughs but after a while you get tired of seeing the same houses, people and settlements. But it doesn't prevent me from coming back to the game after some breaks, because it really is that good. 

That being said I still have not completed all the expansion scenarios and some of them are quite fun and interesting (Last Autumn is a whole different game) so there is some variety to be had there. 

Overall it's an insanely gorgeous and fun game. It has amazing music, city building, ressource management, people management and even child labour! What more could you ask for?

TL;DR Game is good, buy it. Can't wait for 1886.

217217974.review:
I enjoy strategy and base-building games, but Frostpunk goes far beyond managing resources or optimizing layouts. It feels less like a traditional strategy sim and more like a simulation of real leadership economics, politics, and moral decision-making under pressure.

What Frostpunk does exceptionally well is show that you cannot please everyone. Even when you make a rational choice that is clearly necessary for survival, part of your population will see you as a villain. The game forces you to accept that “doing the right thing” often depends on perspective, and that leadership means carrying the consequences of choices that others will resent.

Unlike many strategy games where efficiency is the ultimate goal, Frostpunk makes responsibility the core challenge. Survival is harsh, compromises are unavoidable, and morality is never simple. It’s not just about building a city it’s about understanding the cost of keeping one alive.

Recommended for players who enjoy strategy games that challenge not just their planning skills, but their values and decision-making under pressure.

217077633.review:
I had heard about how difficult this game was, and how important the choices were, and I like RTS and city building so I figured I'd give it a shot.

At a certain point, it felt like there was no way I was going to be able to beat the story mode on my first run through, and this is after the part where the game explicitly tells you: "Look, there isn't anything else you can do except stock up, hunker down, and try to see if you can make it through the storm." I tried to just click button that makes time go by fastest and run it out so I could see what it looked like to lose.

Here comes the hardest part: THE GAME WOULDN'T LET ME LOSE. Turns out food isn't that important because my town ran out of cooked food almost immediately, With only 300 units or so of raw food, my whole community apparently did just fine. I ran out of coal, and the generator was off for what felt like the entirety of the endgame, which is this big bad storm rolling through. My entire community is "Freezing" according to the thermal overlay, and I guess there was like 5 guys who weren't sick or amputated that managed to scrape together enough coal for me to turn on the generator for the last hour where it drops to 100 below zero, and bam, I won. That whole thing probably took an hour of just begging this game to kill me, and it wouldn't. Every 5 seconds or so something happens that slows time down to the default play speed or actually pauses it because someone has to tell you how scary and horrible this storm is, that is apparently not that hard to wait out even with zero food, coal, or heat.

This game would be fun if it didn't force you to sit through an hour of it faking like it was going to kill you, making me think I was going to have to start over and try again, only to actually win. So weird.

217051675.review:
One of the most intense and rewarding strategy games I’ve ever played!

Frostpunk is not a traditional city builder, and that’s the most important thing to understand going in.

This game is about survival under pressure, not optimization or building something pretty. The systems are deep, interconnected, and often intentionally unforgiving. You will make decisions that feel wrong, uncomfortable, or unfair...and that’s the point.

I lost my first serious run after several hours. It stung. But instead of feeling cheap, it felt earned. Frostpunk doesn’t punish you for random reasons; it punishes you for being late, for assuming you can fix everything later, or for trying to save everyone when the world simply won’t allow it.

When it finally clicked, it really clicked.

The moment-to-moment gameplay is slow and thoughtful, but the tension is constant. Heat, food, sickness, hope, and discontent are all quietly working against you. The game does an incredible job of making you feel responsible for every outcome, good or bad. There are no easy “correct” choices; just tradeoffs.

Visually and thematically, the game is outstanding. The art direction, sound design, and music all reinforce the bleak atmosphere without ever becoming overbearing. And the ending of the main scenario is genuinely powerful. It's one of the few games I've played where finishing the game feels emotionally earned.

Important advice for new players:

- Expect to fail.....a lot. That’s normal.
- This is not a “play forever in one city” game by default.
- Pausing, planning ahead, and learning systems matters more than reacting.
- The game rewards understanding, not brute force.

If you enjoy high-stakes strategy, meaningful decisions, and games that respect your intelligence (even when they’re brutal), Frostpunk is absolutely worth your time.

Just don’t go in expecting comfort. Go in expecting consequences.

216723940.review:
I absolutely love this game. If you’re into management games, especially resource management, Frostpunk is an amazing experience. It’s harsh, stressful, and constantly forces you to make tough decisions. There are even higher difficulty settings for people who want it even more brutal.

The DLCs are a huge plus for me. They don’t just add more content, they completely change the perspective of the game. Each one tells its own story and shifts the focus, which changes your goals and how you play. Because of that, you often have to relearn things from scratch – old strategies simply stop working, and you’re forced to adapt. That’s exactly what makes the DLCs so interesting and fun. 

Frostpunk teaches you frustration tolerance, patience, and how to improve through failure.
My very first run was pure hell. I had never played games like this before and didn’t really understand the concept at all. I went in completely blind and failed hard. But once it finally clicked and I started learning from my mistakes, the feeling of actually beating the game was unbelievable. 
That is Frostpunkt!  10/10

216600169.review:
A decent builder game, but one with tension and surprises.

While I do recommend to get the game while it's on sale, I also think the game is a bit overglazed.
Frostpunk does a lot of things right and gives you a nice, atmospheric, survival experience. 

However, I didn't feel that it has particularly good game design. I personally am not a fan of reloading saves to undo my mistake, or lose potentially hours of progress. Some of the challenges and systems can only be understood through trial and error, which is pretty normal. What is not normal, is that the game doesn't prepare you adequately for your next challenge and even punishes you for going in blindly.
The "challenge" of this game feels more akin to "well, here is a problem you aren't prepared for. Lets see if you randomly put yourself into a position where you can handle it or you just die, if you didn't prepare save file ahead of time."
The game simply doesn't give you enough time or chances, to deal with it's "challenges" unless you understand it already, but the problem is, who the hell wants to redo 2-4 hours of progress to understand it better? That puts the player in a dilemma, where they either have to constantly save and reload, which feels lame, or to accept the loss and start over. I find that loop frustrating and repetitive.

This kind of design demotivated me from even trying anything after the main campaign, because I just don't think that's fun. I don't mind failure, I don't mind retrying, I don't mind unpredictability or short loops of gameplay. But when my skill at the game is only determined by "knowing ahead of time, by having failed it before" as everything is scripted, it feels more like the game is asking me to trial and error my way through till I find the right order of actions, instead of finding my own solutions to the problem.

Regardless of my criticism, I still recommend the game for a nice one time novelty experience, as it does have a very immersive atmosphere to it. I did feel "achieved" for beating the game, but did not particularly like how I was punished in ways, that I cannot prevent without knowing better before playing.

Still a solid 7/10.

One of the most interesting strategy games I've played. Every  element of this -- music, lore, the mechanical representations of that lore, scenario setup, visual design -- is supporting every other element like a giant dome made up of a dozen different struts. The radial building and heating mechanics are both novel and work really well in tandem. The event chains are pretty simple, but they do a ton of additional work by implication. There is no part of this game that feels superfluous, and there's no point where I didn't feel like the commander of a doomed Artic outpost on the verge of either freezing to death or being cannibalized by my mutinous crew. 
For five bucks, v worth it. I'd get the DLCs tbh

216421114.review:
I tried Frostpunk in lets say late 2022, learned the mechanics after a few good efforts, failed many times, figured out what I learned was actually dumb and suboptimal, got 80% done through the New Home scenario and then stopped playing for 3 years. Then I came back 2025 and tried again. 

My second round things were much smoother. I will note that relearning to play the game was much more enjoyable than going in blind, things were familiar enough I didn't feel like hitting a stump but distant enough to where I could still get that rush of succeeding in my explorations. And then once I kept playing, I got *really* into Frostpunk.

It is cold. Awfully cold in how easily left in the dust you can get once your mistakes start to pile. Often I would see myself on the path towards glorious success with a city I'm proud to have created only to then realize how deeply unprepared I've actually been. People start getting sick, medical posts filled to the brim, workers on leave unable to supply my resources, coal reserves dry up leaving my homes unheated, it cycles into a cold miserable drawl where you look at how you started to where you've landed and can only pinpoint the exact moment your city was doomed. And that pin is much earlier than you think it is.

Not that all of Frostpunk is just dying in the snow! Its also a lot about your success, your optimizations, your progression from suffering to surviving to as close as you can get to thriving which is still so far away. When you sit back looking at the systems laid out by your own hand, seeing the time pass and your resources growing, seeing the hope instilled in your people, those are joys found in such a cold cold game. 

Anyways! Music is thematically such a banger, creates that tension perfect for the crapshow you're in, that desperation, that uncertainty of if you can succeed. Or the quieter moments, when you can only look at your people, watching them work away through the snow, an odd sense of peace you get from it. Building designs, just the design of everything I just like to look it. So nicely done. So clean and frosty. 

Enjoy a city builder challenge with cold themes and beautiful soundtracks? You know what game to play.
