The game seemed promising at first; a strategy game without combat felt like an interesting change of pace for me. The game looks good and has many positive elements. However, I can’t hide my frustration and must simply say that the game is a complete waste of time. Melancholy, death, ♥♥♥♥, illness, hunger, and nothing but bad choices. In this sense the game is very realistic, because humanity probably wouldn’t survive in these conditions in real life either… and I’m not even sure the game has any other ending than being banished. It’s mostly a question of when that happens.

Starving people don’t eat, the sick don’t go to hospitals, and people assigned to work don’t actually do what they’re supposed to. They certainly complain, though. When you build multiple hospitals with very limited resources and assign everyone available to hunt for food, the only reward you get is more complaints. I don’t know if this is a bug or if I did something wrong, but temperatures were fine and there were workers in the cookhouse as well. There are also far too few people, and even when you find more through scouting, the hunters still don’t bring in enough food to sustain them. Two coal mines and a coal thumper aren’t enough to keep the generator running once you start upgrading it.

I spent some time studying the game on YouTube, because the game itself offers virtually no guidance at all. Workshop upgrades are extremely insignificant and didn’t seem to have any real impact, let alone speed up resource gathering. After learning the mechanics, the game managed to stay balanced on a knife’s edge for a few hours, but once the story started throwing constant setbacks in your face, everything fell apart and there were no longer any means to fix the situation. Restarting a slow-paced game over and over again completely drained my motivation.

215078339.review:
I've never bothered to review a single game in my 16 years on Steam until now.
What can I say about Frostpunk... well I thought this is just another city builder by an indie company... no.
Starting the campaign 'A new home' can be frustrating at first. Within a few days the temperature drops to a staggering -40 degrees (rookie numbers in game), and you really have to learn to micromanage and use the pause button. Once you get the hang of it, its a case of manage how you want, try to be as kind or cruel to be kind to your population as you desire. There are many ways to do this but you basically need to get everything sorted for around day 40 because the big freeze is coming, the great storm... -130 degrees. Carbon Dioxide freezes at -79. Ethanol at -114. We're only 50 degrees above the freezing point of Oxygen. The population is panicking, they can't work, they're freezing in their homes and in the last few moments you're praying tens or hundreds don't perish... the music progressively gets more intense (and it's a gorgeous soundtrack btw).
Finally, you pull through, the generator hasn't exploded, the screen turns dark. These guys only show you an entire time-lapse of your city's journey. Mind blown. 630 made it. 
10/10. Will get DLCs and probably collectors/deluxe edition just to support these guys and for the sweet soundtrack.

215031345.review:
[h1] This game is a gem. [/h1]

Frostpunk disguises itself as a logistics puzzle: heat curves, coal ratios, sick-to-worker math. You’re punished brutally for inefficiency, so your brain adapts. You stop thinking in faces and start thinking in flows. Food in, coal out, sickness minimized. And the game rewards that mindset.

[h3] I thought, this game was never about survival, was it? [/h3]

You become competent, disciplined, decisive. A leader who never lets the city idle.

But Frostpunk is quietly watching how you win.

At some point, without a single clear moment of choice, the line between protecting people and controlling them disappears.

Yes, please, get the game, or play it again. Do your best to survive. You will not regret it.

215029281.review: Despite loving strategy and civ building games i somehow never considered FrostPunk. I gues i thought it was something different? Anyway i stumbled upon a random video of someone doing a 'child labour only challenge' and watching that video was simultaneously the best and worst decision of my life. As someone who always plays the hero in games, here you have to do some awful things to get the job done. Somehow that is the main appeal for me, having to make hard decisions i've never needed to make in all my years playing survival games where consequences were mine alone. Kindness doesn't exist here, you either manipulate the masses with religion or go full dictator on those ungrateful mongrels.

215024344.review:
Good old frostpunk. Its a VERY hard game, even just starting out and on the easy difficulties you will have problems until you start to understand some of the more "optimized" layouts of a city. I come back to this occasionally and play through the missions again, or try achievement hunting. 

I gotta say though, while this may not be everyones cup of tea, the atmosphere and experience of this game will be in my top 3 gaming experiences for a while. The music, the grittyness, the stress, and the accomplishment that comes with it is just incredible. For me specifically there was an extra layer that probably added to my first experience. So when loading the game, the like splash menu brands that come up is one for alienware of all things. Which sounds ridiculous to anyone that doesnt have alienware stuff. I only did at the time because covid made graphics cards more expensive that prebuilt pc's so i had an alienware pc back then. One of the coolest factors on a first playthrough was the alienware rgb being linked to the temperature in game. First starting out all the rgbs are in the orange/yellow/red range, but when a storm hits, or a major temperature drop happens all of a sudden your lighting all fade into darker and darker shades of blue. Couple that with having a window AC in the game room that when left on just made the room colder as it went on. TALK ABOUT AN UNMATCHED FIRST EXPERIENCE. Was actually insane, i was getting cold while my village was, and while my temperature didnt change when a storm passed, there was a bit of immersion happening and relief i had when all the lights around me change back to their warmer hue. 

Its a weird thing to find immersive, but its something ive not had in any other game, and something that im sure very few people have even experienced with this game unless you were like me and had an alienware at the time. I eventually built a new pc so my stuff doesnt react like it used to, but i remember the feeling all the same. 

Not to dog on Frostpunk 2, its great in its own right. I was really hype for its release, but theres something different between when a cold snap hits and you lose 15 of your valuable people, and having a megacity that results in losing hundreds, but yet feels like it doesnt impact you so much. Frostpunk 1 just feels more personal in a way which always keeps me coming back

is this game fun? to an Addictive extent (i literally just stayed up until 6am playing it)
does it also include heavy-handed anti-communist perspectives? unfortunately, yes.
did they make a convincing argument against it? absolutely not. fell flat on its face. 
i built a city in which every single citizen's needs were met. the hungry were fed. the sick were treated. the amputated were given new limbs. the cold were given warm homes (yes, even during the Peak of the final storm). i didn't even need to silence the masses or terrorize them into compliance, i didn't cover up poetry that criticized my regime, nor did i hurt or kill people that threatened the status quo.

my Only regret is needing to make use of many 24 hour emergency shifts, as i never had the opportunity to build an automaton. 


at the end of the first story, the one i just completed, a cinematic displayed the evolution of my city.
it ended on the words "your city survived, but at what cost?" after repremanding me for building watchtowers that citizens Wanted, a propaganda house that gave people hope when they had None, and a prison to keep literal Vandals, Thieves and Murderers.

at what cost did my city survive? that's a good question. was it people's freedom of expression? uhhhh No. the cost was occasionally having to work for 24 hours straight. because the world was Ending and we were all gonna die if the work didn't get done. 

the storytellers don't seem to care about violating workers' rights, though



is this a helpful review of the game known as Frostpunk?
nah i don't think so. i just pulled an all-nighgter and i chose to take a dump on this story's ending because i thought it was Stoopid and should've been better written.

do i think frostpunk is a game Not worth playing? hell no this ♥♥♥♥ was still so much fun. oh how i love feeding the hungry and giving warm homes to the poor.
i think the real world should work like that too. minus the. occasional 24 hour emergency shifts.
good fecken thing we already have automata ready to pick up some the work.




if by chance someone is actually reading this rant all the way through, i would like to apologize for any broken grammar, sentence structure and/or choice of words that make me sound insane. that's because yesterday night i got drunk at an 18th birthday party. take a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ sip, babes.

214170259.review:
[h1]8.5/10 Not for everyone, but one of the best city sims I have played[/h1]

The game is really unforgiving, you will fail unless you watched a video or read a guide on your first attempt on pretty much every scenario(The arks you can first attempt tbh). It will be so frustrating to see many of your people get ill, starve, homeless or have hope plummet due to a situation out of your control, but that is the nature of this game. It's almost like a roguelike in the sense that each time you fail and do another run you learned something new, improving on your previous attempt. Events are sporadic, a new one comes after another some will tank your entire run in an instant, and with that failure you learn as you begin to prepare for said event, but others will give you just enough to survive sending some form of relief. With all this happening in your own city, you will face multiple moral choices that will really test how you run a society with so little, can you help others if you can't even help yourself? It's a great game but you either have to be A. Into city sims, B. A masochist, C. Both; Otherwise I'm not quite sure how well this game will fair for you, I think other games would serve as better introductory city sim games due to this games difficulty or if you are going to play this as your first city sim at least play on the easy difficulty/watch a guide before diving in.  That being said once you get the hang of the scenarios I don't it's particularly hard, even on the harder difficulties, it's a steep learning curve that plateaus. The game also has an awesome soundtrack, atmosphere, and general art/sound direction with the steampunk aesthetic blending so well with the freezing environment.

214128328.review:
This is the game that made me a fan of 11bit studios. What a modern classic. As a city builder, this game is pretty bare bones. There are also more complex management simulators, survival games, and campaign driven experiences out there.

But this game manages to uniquely blend all of those elements into an unforgettable experience. The feeling that you get while playing, being inspired by the ingenuity and perseverance of the human spirit against a bleak and novel apocalypse, is unmatched. The soundtrack is incredible and the song that plays at the climax of the main scenario brings a tear to my eye.

One of my all-time favorites. Highly recommend.

214112088.review:
Frostpunk is one of my first experiences with city planners, and I honestly felt quite eager to try the genre out since it appeared more story driven and I liked the look of the art style. I however felt disappointed in the story and how expansive it was and felt the narrative aspect didn’t influence the gameplay much, basically only happening for what felt like very minor things, and all the citizens just felt like random people because of it, where it didn’t matter who did what or what happened to specific citizens.

I felt the greater overarching story carries the game better and allows for a good ramp up and makes it feel like progressing the city is worthwhile, but I also felt a lack of freedom to plan the city due to the constraints the game puts on how you are able to build in order to be able to access certain resources and the game softly forcing you to build only in the way that gets the optimal results. This may be an aspect of city builders some find positive, but I felt it lacked the freedom of expression and strategy, allowing you to just do everything as long as you do it as the devs intended feels plain and lacking in variety.

The art and music for the game at least make for an atmospheric package that allowed me to still find some enjoyment in the game. I personally wouldn’t recommend this game as an intro or as a city planner game; however, this game frequently gets heavily discounted, and if you choose to pick it up I’d recommend doing it within one of those timeframes, otherwise not at all.

214023335.review:
A survival epic and a must play for all the Shackleton fans out there who insist that "no one has to die". If you think you might like it, you will. Frankly, it is the benchmark for a lot of other imitators in a genre that, if I'm not mistaken, it created.
Most of what it does, it does very well. There are some annoyances. I don't ever need to play the Winterhome scenario again simply due to the sheer amount of clicking necessary to slowly clear out the burned areas of the city. With that said, the ending was worth the trouble, and of the endings, the best story Frostpunk has to tell is the main one, backed up by a great soundtrack. It's done right. Really nails the tense themes and escalating dread as danger closes in. I think the main case against playing Frostpunk now is just that there is a remake on the horizon, but that just goes to show you that the strength of the core material here is compelling.

Playing the sequel made me realize how special this game is. It is a great and unique survival experience. You get to place the individual buildings, research and improve your town, do meaningful expeditions, care for your citizens while juggling and managing lots of resources in a harsh winter while getting to make awful political decisions to keep everyone alive. It's amazing, it's immersive and it hooks you emotionally to the city and your journey.

Honorable mention to the Last Autumn expansion. What a great DLC it was. It gives enough of a similar experience from the campaign while also introducing fresh objectives with their own challenges and gives you a glimpse of the lore and the world before the winter.

213907959.review:
Tl;Dr - [b]Not beginner friendly in the slightest.[/b] I can see why people like this, but the tutorial doesn't communicate things properly. As a result, the player is punished. I had a friend watch me play, and it constantly shifted from "this is an interesting concept" to "I have no idea what I'm doing." 

There were moments where both of us realized the game hadn't explained a certain concept, and my friend had to specifically tell me where to go. My rule of casual gaming is always "if there's no tutorial for it, I don't know it exists." While yes, there are tutorial elements, I did not disable the popup tutorial feature, so I presumed if I did something new or was able to do something new, the game would explain to me. This was not the case. 

Because things weren't properly explained, here is a variety of things that happened:
- Ended up wasting half a day with no work being done because I wasn't told to "build a road."
- Invested in a coal thumper, thinking it would actually produce coal, turns out it just produced coal PILES.
- People need better healthcare, but I don't know what else to do other than build more medical tents, but I only have a handful of people to run it.
- Didn't know how to pursue "research" until my friend told me to build the workshop. That should be in the tutorial AFTER learning how to resource gather.

213901307.review: You can order children to work in the mines or keep them in orphanage as unpaid labors, harvest death people organ in a snow pit for cheaper medical costs or burry them in proper cemetery paid by hard-earned wood, coal and steel resources that could be better be invested in housing or farm. Fronk Punk is the kind of game that just teaches you to be humble with power, especially when your subjects can only do one thing: complains endlessly about how they are starving and people get sick everywhere, and coal is running out, and the generator - yeah, that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ generator - just stood there and watches you suffer trying to feed power to it. I think the genius of Frostpunk is how COMITTED it is to the premise: the central generator goes down, everyone dies. That's it! Every bad to worse decisions you end up making after that just validates the point the game trying to make a power: in the end, it's just a useful thing people kill each other for in hard times! Gameplay-wise, Frost Punk truly does not hold your hands. It show you a couple tutorials for you to handle buttons and features before leave you alone to suffer your society collapse. :) 8/10, though I believe the core gameplay could be expanded, but like I said: Frost Punk make a name for itself by sticking faithfully to the premise: the generator goes down, everyone dies. Enjoy!

The music and beautiful graphics will keep you immersed throughout the entire storyline. The harsh choices will make you reflect on how despair pushes our moral boundaries and you will be a changed person after this game.

213680730.review:
⭐ 10/10 – I Passed Child Labor Laws and Still Felt Bad About It ⭐

Frostpunk is a city builder that doesn’t ask “Can you survive?” so much as “How much of your soul are you willing to burn to do it?” And the answer, it turns out, is quite a lot — especially when it’s −60°C and the generator is coughing ominously.

You start with hope, optimism, and a very reasonable plan. A few hours later, you’re signing laws you swore you never would, staring at a coal deficit, and convincing yourself that extended shifts are actually a form of teamwork. The game constantly forces hard choices, and it’s disturbingly good at making you justify them.

The atmosphere is unmatched. The sound design, music, and visuals work together to create a sense of constant dread, broken only by brief moments of triumph when the city survives one more night. Watching the generator glow in the frozen darkness is both beautiful and deeply stressful.

Frostpunk isn’t just about building a city — it’s about managing people, morale, and the consequences of leadership. Every victory feels earned. Every loss feels personal. And every successful run makes you question what kind of ruler you’ve become.

If you enjoy strategy games that emotionally punish you while still being incredibly rewarding, Frostpunk is a masterpiece.

Highly recommended. The city must survive. Even if you’re not sure it should.

213675442.review: Fun in the freezing cold. Who's ready for some soup?

213663848.review:
I bought this game in 2020 and have yet to complete a full run, but every time I return to it, I feel more and more confident in my skills - and more and more bruising to my hubris. This game is PUNISHING. The more I play it, the more I want to get it RIGHT, and getting it "right" isn't easy! I have the "benefit" of knowledge (i.e. I have watched playthroughs), but to be honest, I would recommend going in blind. You will struggle! You will curse every decision you make! But that's the point. Play it through the way you want to, don't look up too much information or guidance, and give yourself the chance to make choices and experience the ramifications therein. Because every choice you make is going to have its benefits and its downsides. Those results will influence how much you get out of this game.

If that isn't enough to persuade you: I am an artist, and my next exhibition has been deeply inspired by this game. Exhibitions can take years to complete, but I'm devoting myself to the work because the elements I want to explore - ones I got from Frostpunk - are so powerful. Frostpunk raises important questions, and while it doesn't always answer those questions, I have still been motivated to tackle them myself. So this game is affecting a big part of my career right now!

Do the main scenario! Do the alternate scenarios! Do it on normal mode, or hard mode, or Endless mode, or even easy mode! Frostpunk works as well as it does because it balances its story with its mechanics. Its format as a game serves its story very well, and as such, I can recommend it highly, even having not completed the main scenario.

213659728.review:
I don't play that many citybuilders, but I watched a review about this game by a certain Bricky on YouTube, and I found it quite funny, and the game seemed interesting. Furthermore, some friend of mine recommended it to me, so I bought it...

Boy oh boy am I glad I bought the game. So, the soundtrack is great (specially The City Must Survive, which hits like a train), the game is a bit hard to master at first, but it is intentional, you are building the last city on Earth during a catastrophic global icing, it doesn't sound easy in the slightest, does it? The game has a bunch of scenarios you can play, and a sandbox mode to do what you like. You will spend most of your time playing the scenarios, so it makes sense to discuss them in depth.

The scenarios ARE Frostpunk, they flesh out the world and give you great stories to go through. Even though it's a game, you will feel forced to make harsh decisions, and feel bad when you see your citizens suffer due to them. You could also ignore the story if you have limited time and want to do a run as fast as possible, it won't detract anything from the gameplay point of view.

So, great gameplay, great soundtrack, great story, no bugs that I can recall, the game might struggle a bit if you play it for too long (but you won't experience massive performance drops), it just feels like it has a whole bunch of love behind it. It's hard, and it's depressing, it's Frostpunk babyyyyyy

12/10 you can introduce child labour as your first law in the game

213651048.review:
Having remained quiet (review-wise) during the last couple of years in which I've absolutely loved playing Frostpunk 1... I now feel compelled to declare my love towards it - publicly - after feeling heartbroken with regard to my experience with Frostpunk 2. Don't get me wrong... I'm not saying that the second game is bad... I'm sure it's a great game, but it's not anything like the first one. I feel like they are different genres. 

Here's what I loved the most about Frostpunk 1:
- it feels 'intimate'.  Every single worker (or engineer) counts. You don't have that many, in total, in the entire playthrough. It feels personal. 
- I usually dislike games in which you micro-manage stuff/individual.. but I absolutely love that in Frostpunk 1. Mainly because it's never overwhelming, and it does make a difference. 
- a playthrough feels "small"... in a really good way. There is not a giant learning curve ahead... you can figure out stuff mostly by yourself (without ALT+TAB+google) on easier difficulties... yes, while struggling a lot, and maybe with some save-loading... but... you'll do it and then you can come back the second time stronger (and on a higher difficulty), you'll absolutely nail it - and, you'll feel good about it. 

PLEASE: release more DLC for Frostpunk 1! I'll buy them, I promise!

It's nice to have a city builder with a strong theme and a main scenario that ends. Finishing the scenario feels satisfying, and still leaves enough mechanics and strategies untouched that there's room to try something new in a different scenario or a second run.

11bit's design philosophy of allowing the gameplay to portray the narrative tensions shows through. The worldbuilding and flavor is strong, but you don't need to pay attention to every detail to get what it's trying to convey. Ultimately the decisions you're making as the manager of the city drives the story forward. You're always going in the same direction, but you can tweak the way you get there.

213293140.review:
[b] Pros: [/b]
It's a steampunk city builder sim with (optional) cannibalism, child labor, using the dead as fertilizer, either total religious fanatism or just straight up totalitarian fascism. You can also send a 7-year-old into a reactor core to fix it (you rock lil Timmy, also RIP lil Timmy). The weather gets closer to absolute zero than the freezing point of water on certain days and I don't think anyone shower for months on end. It can get very hard managing an overwhelming amount of stockpiles, people's morale and prioritizing the right resources, but this kind of planning ahead and micromanaging every little detail is just what I want from a stressful city builder. Music and the well crafted sound effects are superb and really add to the immersion.

[b] Cons: [/b]
You have to manage a town full of British people.

212708146.review:
Frostpunk is a post-apocalyptic strategy-based resource-based city survival and builder with quite a unique premise and some major potential. The gameplay is unique and exciting, with random events occurring if you're playing a scenario, and also offers a more sandbox-oriented "endless" mode.

Playtime: 10.2 hours (PC, M&K)

[b]Overall:  7.5/10[/b]

[u]Narrative: 6.5/10[/u] - Frostpunk can only be slightly classified as a narrative-based game, as the "story mode" is only one scenario out of several available. I only played the main story ("A New Home"). I've played quite a few city survival sims and Frostpunk is one of the only ones that has attempted to include an actual narrative into the game. I won't say it's anything groundbreaking, though the story does tie in well to the gameplay. Though I did find the inclusion of a story and random citizen events a cool addition to the game, it did feel like the story and the decisions that the player makes with regard to the story, have little to minimal actual effect on the game or the outcome of a game. Of course, I did only play the main story, and I can see how some other scenarios could improve in this department, such as the refugee one. Frostpunk has quite an interesting world within its narrative and I think I will eventually play some of the other scenarios and probably some "endless" mode (which nixes the story entirely, it seems).

[u]Visuals: 8/10[/u] - Frostpunk has some very nice theming and a unique visual style that helps to set this game apart from other city sims. Obviously, snow and ice is a big part of the visuals in Frostpunk which is done well without making the player suffer from white blindness. Nothing looks totally static in this game, including the frozen wastelands, which is a feat in itself. Character and asset models look good and well-polished. Weather events look good and offer an increasingly desolate and hopeless vibe to the game.

[u]Audio: 8.5/10[/u] - The soundtrack is great and unique/interesting enough to not feel repetitive in a playthrough. Character voices and other sound effects are also done well.

[u]Gameplay: 8.5/10[/u] - Frostpunk's gameplay is generally fun, exciting, and keeps the player on their toes. The sun seems to be dying and is engulfing the planet in ice and freezing cold, and the player is tasked with building a concentric city around a big generator while the cold keeps getting colder. I liked the expanded story capabilities brought by scouts and outpost teams, offering the player a glimpse into the outside world and some hints at the grand mystery of why this is all happening. Aside from tech upgrades which allow you to keep surviving in an ever-colder winter, there exists a policy book in which the player may change laws at a whim, particularly when disaster approaches. I only played the main story, so I only saw the order and faith policies, but I wonder if other scenarios offer different policy books. The player must balance rushing tech upgrades when needed and building houses and other structures to enable better resource collection. There are several different scenarios and the inclusion of an "endless" mode (which basically seems to nix the story in favor of a more sandbox-type gameplay. A couple of things that I think could be improved are the tutorials, which could be expanded upon slightly to provide the player with a more solid understanding of the game mechanics out of the gate, and the player being more-or-less an autocratic king with citizen desires being basically an afterthought. I've seen some teases for Frostpunk 2 and it seems this was improved upon greatly. Overall, it's a good city management sim that isn't overly punishing for small mistakes (the player can scrape by while being a little inefficient), and offers some sandbox-type gameplay.

[u]Character Design: -/10[/u]

[u]Environment and Level Design: 7/10[/u] - There are several different maps in Frostpunk, each tied to a certain scenario, but also are selectable in the "endless" mode, which allows the player to pick whatever map they want as long as they aren't playing a scenario. I only played on the first map (tied to A New Home), but the other maps look cool and offer their own challenges and opportunities. Small bundles of resources are available to collect by hand in the beginning and I found these to be balanced well.

[u]Personal Engagement: 9/10[/u]

[u]Personal Replay Value: 9/10[/u]

[u]Performance: 10/10[/u] - 2k144, no performance issues. No bugs encountered.

[i]i5-13600KF; RTX 4070 Super 12GB; 32GB RAM[/i]

212597812.review:
My goodness.. what an incredible game. It's far from perfect, rarely ever does a title so complex do everything without some level of imperfections. But what is present remains polished, balanced, and difficult in a fair sense. I can't recommend Frostpunk enough, especially to newcomers of the Resource Management / Builder genre. The tutorial is very detailed and simple - but with a fail state around every corner, making the act of teaching you one with consequences and risk.

You will have moments where your last ~10% of stored coal are all you have to survive the next five hours. You'll test the patience of your population as you attempt to balance dissonance with food, medicine, and warmth. Every action you make has a long-lasting consequence that can bite you five hours into your session. This is by far the most high-risk game I've ever played in my life. Asking the player to have forethought, common sense, and even a hint of clairvoyance to predict what the future holds is something few - if any - modern games will do. Dark Souls sends you back to a bonfire. Frostpunk sends you back three hours to remedy a poor decision you made or to the main menu for a fresh start.

And when you couple the incredibly tense, exciting, stressful gameplay with the beautiful music, artwork, graphics, and building design.. it just makes for a borderline perfect game. Everything you interact with feels akin to playing with small-scale homes inside of an arts & crafts store such as Michaels or Hobby Lobby. Even the sound design of building objects has a plasticy wooden effect that really stimulates your brain.

Play it on a weekend because you will lose the next 2 to 3 days of your life.

This is the only game I have ever played that deserves the "Overwhelmingly positive" review title.

Regarding Linux - there is one issue present. If you check ProtonDB most people state they rolled back to Proton 6.x or 8.x - however I recommend staying on the latest Proton-GE (10-25 as of writing). The issue present is clicking "Play" results in the game not launching but rather stalling with no signs of life. [b]Let it stall[/b] - it will eventually launch after ~30 minutes (7800X3D & nVME SSD) - and it will not present the issue again afterwards, so there's no need to fret.

[h2] Overview [/h2]
After about 16 hours with Frostpunk on PC, I found it to be a tough but rewarding survival city-builder.
The constant pressure of managing heat, resources, and morale keeps the game tense, and the choices you make through the law system feel meaningful and sometimes harsh.

[h2] Gameplay & Atmosphere [/h2]
The frozen setting looks great, and the atmosphere really sells the struggle. It can feel overwhelming at first, but once you get used to the systems, stabilizing your city feels very satisfying. Performance on PC was smooth, with no issues during intense moments.

[h3] Pros [/h3]
[b]+[/b] Deep, meaningful decisions
[b]+[/b] Strong atmosphere and visuals
[b]+[/b] Challenging and rewarding gameplay

[h3] Cons [/h3]

210952382.review:
Frostpunk — Leadership, Sacrifice, and Survival in a Frozen World

Frostpunk thrusts players into a brutally cold, post-apocalyptic world where humanity’s last hope lies in a single city built around a massive generator. As the leader, you’ll face impossible decisions, balancing survival, morality, and the needs of your people as temperatures plummet and resources vanish. From the first moment, the game establishes a grim yet compelling tone that rarely lets up.

The game shines in its city-building and survival mechanics. Every choice matters—where you place buildings, how you manage coal, food, and shelter, and how you prepare for the next deadly cold wave. The pressure never fades, and the sense of responsibility is constant. The laws you enact, whether compassionate or ruthless, shape both the city and its people in lasting ways.

Decision-making is the beating heart of Frostpunk. The moral dilemmas are powerful, often forcing you to choose between efficiency and humanity. Will you build hope, enforce order, or sacrifice ethics for survival? Each route offers unique challenges and emotional weight, making every playthrough feel personal and impactful.

Atmosphere and presentation are exceptional. The stark, snow-covered landscapes, detailed city animations, and haunting music create a chilling yet beautiful world. The visual storytelling—fading light, frostbitten buildings, and workers huddling for warmth—adds depth to the struggle of your citizens and the sacrifices they endure.

[h3]Final Verdict:[/h3]
Frostpunk is a masterful blend of strategy, survival, and moral storytelling. Challenging, emotional, and deeply immersive, it stands as one of the most compelling city-building experiences ever made. Whether you rule with compassion or cold logic, the journey will stay with you long after the generator’s heat fades.

“A cold, unforgiving, and unforgettable tale of leadership and survival.”

Overall Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐☆☆ 8/10

209891558.review: The best survival city builder game ever. I've spent most of my time playing on the Xbox version. The atmosphere, art style, & core game cycle all combine faultlessly. In fact, I had never seen a minor bug, let alone a game-breaking issue. In our times, when games are released broken, unplayable, & are unfinished, it's so good to see a company that makes & releases functional, finished games. I'd still recommend waiting until it goes on sale and getting all three DLCs at once. 11 Bit Studios also does DLC mostly right. The Last Autumn DLC completely changes how you approach the game. It could've been a separate game, but it isn't; it adds a lot to the base game & should always be bought with it. The other two DLC are mediocre & don't really add much, however, they are priced somewhat reasonably. It's a challenging game, but it's not impossible to beat. You are meant to play it multiple times, learning from your mistakes & slowly mastering it. This is how all strategy games should work, especially survival city builders. TL;TR, it just works.

209622665.review:
Holy crap. It's difficult to make a game that creates so much tension that the player is at the edge of their seat, hyper-focused, fully engaged, invested, and locked-in. It's even more difficult to do that with the genres of city-building and survival, but, dang; 11 bit studios not only pulls it off but smashes it out of the park, delivering a truly intense gaming experience despite the genre.

Gameplay is immensely satisfying, challenging, and gratifying. Strategic placement of each individual structure is key to successfully maximizing your resources, which just affords a level of strategic depth to rival even the most tactical and strategic games. A wide array of difficulty settings and game modes makes the game surprisingly accessible even to more casual players, though the game was definitely intended for the more invested.

But where this game really shines is purely in the aesthetic, atmosphere, and story-telling, even though it is not particularly narrative-driven. I'm already a bit of a sucker for steampunk, but I'm also a sucker for the narrative feature of battling harsh and supernaturally overwhelming elements, and this marries the two perfectly. Furthermore, the technology and policy choices really lend themselves to the idea of abandoning moral norms in the name of survival.

(Vanilla) Frostpunk deserves to go down as not only one of the greatest city-builders of all time, but one of the greatest spliced-genre games of all time. Especially if you pick this game up on sale, the value is unbeatable, and the scenarios are wildly unique, diverse, and engaging. Wrap all this up in amazing and artfully-applied graphics and an epic score, and you've got an unforgettable gaming experience. Not sure I can say enough good about this game.

I give this game a thumbs up to buy when on sale.

I nearly gave it a thumbs down, because the game simply abandons realism. The temperatures depicted, the enviroment conditions, etc. All of it make everything we do in the game impossible. The main generator itself would cease to function beyond -60c, and the game goes all the way down to -120c

I was dissapointed by the lack of realism from this developer who made This War of Mine. I won't be buying Frostpunk 2.

But in a vacuum, this game is fun to play. Science-nerds like me who really love phyiscs in this Universe in which we live, won't enjoy the core tenants of basic realism being completely abandoned for the sake of pure story telling impact, which for people like me serves to destroy the immersion, thus lessening the impact of the story.

Even the music is too much, I had to turn it off. It's constantly making the game sound like something majorly impactful is happening, when most of the time it's just another normal day.

Peace.

208814435.review:
Frostpunk – Pros & Cons
Pros:
Story is strong and emotionally engaging.
Graphics are gorgeous and fit the bleak setting perfectly.
Music and sound design are excellent and enhance immersion.
The amount of detail is impressive (workers pushing snow, heat zones, etc.).
Weather system is dynamic and adds depth to the survival challenge.
UI is minimalistic but clear and functional.
The atmosphere makes the world genuinely feel cold.
Difficulty encourages thoughtful planning and tough decision-making.
The Law system allows interesting moral and social paths (even harsh ones).
Storytelling rewards paying attention instead of handing everything to the player.
Cons:
Can be unforgiving for new players.
Game length is short, with limited scenarios.
Lack of modding support reduces replayability.
Overall:
Frostpunk is a powerful and immersive city-survival experience with amazing atmosphere and meaningful choices. However, more game modes and mod support would make its longevity even better. It remains a standout title worth playing.

I wanted to love Frostpunk but between some questionable execution, demanding mechanics+citizens, and a scheduled Disaster that the game doesn't warn you about but also has at the exact same time every time (why? it doesn't incentivize you to be prepared for disasters in general, just plan around specific scripted events, while still being painful for no real payoff). I don't think it's a terrible game - it's got things to love and you can see that people do love it - but it really isn't for me.

(What I wanted from Frostpunk, I eventually got from Against the Storm, which has a similar idea of colony+base building in a limited, hostile environment, but goes both broader with the mechanic and deeper by making it cyclical on several levels, meaning messing up doesn't mean a complete game over. I sometimes think about trying Frostpunk again, but nothing it has is more appealing to me than just about everything in AtS. Take that as you will.)

208712515.review:
Frostpunk is a survival city-building strategy game developed by 11 bit studios. It challenges players to manage the last city on Earth after a global freeze, balancing efficiency, morality, and survival. Every decision carries weight, forcing compromise between compassion and necessity.

Gameplay revolves around resource management and city planning. Players gather coal, wood, and steel to keep a central generator running—the city’s only heat source. Expansion is constant tension: spreading too far wastes fuel, staying too close risks stagnation. Each day brings new hardships, from blizzards to food shortages.

The law and hope systems define the game’s moral complexity. Players enact laws that shape society—child labor, food rationing, emergency shifts—each carrying moral and psychological costs. The balance between order and hope determines the colony’s endurance more than any resource.

Visually, Frostpunk captures despair and resilience. The frozen wasteland contrasts sharply with the warm glow of the generator. Buildings radiate light against endless snow, giving the city a fragile beauty. The weather effects and lighting reinforce the sense of isolation and urgency.

Sound design deepens immersion. The creaking of ice, the hum of the generator, and the haunting orchestral soundtrack all sustain the tone of endurance against extinction. The audio reinforces every choice—the quiet aftermath of death and the cold wind of survival.

The campaign delivers a contained but powerful narrative, with multiple scenarios expanding replay value. Each scenario redefines priorities—exploration, faith, or order—and tests different management styles. The difficulty remains high, but victory feels earned rather than scripted.

Overall, Frostpunk is a city builder about survival, sacrifice, and the limits of morality. It’s less about expansion and more about preservation. Beautiful, brutal, and unforgettable, it stands as one of the few strategy games that forces players to question not how they build—but what they’re willing to do to keep the lights on.

208625517.review:
How to Make Masala Dosa, 

🌾 Phase 1: Preparation (a.k.a. Resource Gathering)

Total time: 10–12 hours (fermentation is passive, so playtime ≈ unlimited)

You can start this before launching the game — think of it as pre-building your generator.

Ingredients (a.k.a. Basic Resources)

2 cups raw rice (idli/parboiled if possible)

½ cup urad dal (split black gram)

2 tbsp chana dal

1 tsp fenugreek seeds (for fermentation boost — like a steam core)

¼ cup flattened rice (poha)

Water (as needed)

Salt (to taste)

Oil or ghee (for cooking)

For the filling (tech upgrade: Potato Masala)

4 boiled potatoes

2 sliced onions

2 green chilies

1 tsp each mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal

8–10 curry leaves

¼ tsp turmeric

2 tbsp oil

Salt, coriander, lemon juice

🔧 Step 1: Set up the Generator (Fermentation Phase)

While your city generator warms up, set your dosa generator to ferment.

Soak rice, dals, and methi in warm water for 6 hours (this is your “resource collection”).

Just before you start a new Frostpunk scenario, grind them all with poha into a smooth, slightly thick batter.

Keep the batter in a large bowl; cover and ferment overnight.

Pro tip: The batter ferments best in warm weather, or near your PC’s exhaust fan — yes, that GPU heat is finally useful.

Playtime window: 8–12 hours — perfect for surviving an entire storm cycle or two.

🔥 Step 2: Maintain Hope (Prepare Potato Masala)

When your people are freezing and demanding better laws, multitask to maintain both hope and hunger.

While Frostpunk loads or during slower night phases, heat oil in a pan.

Add mustard seeds — wait for them to splutter (like discontent levels rising).

Add urad dal and chana dal; fry till golden.

Throw in curry leaves, green chilies, and grated ginger — this is your “motivation speech.”

Add onions, sauté until translucent (your “generator stress level”).

Add turmeric, salt, and mashed potatoes. Mix well.

Splash a little water, cover for 3–4 minutes.

Add coriander and lemon juice. Keep warm.

Now, leave the masala aside while you handle Londoners.

🧊 Step 3: Survive the Cold (Cook the Dosa)

You’ll need quick reactions here — both for your dosa and your frostbitten workers.

Preheat your tawa (griddle) on medium-high.

It’s like powering up a new coal generator: too hot and it burns, too cold and nothing happens.

Grease lightly with oil or ghee.

Pour a ladle of fermented batter in the center; spread it thinly in circles.

Drizzle oil along the edges.

While it cooks:

Switch to Frostpunk and assign workers to gather coal.

Come back in ~1 minute (when the dosa edges turn brown).

Place a spoonful of potato masala in the middle, fold, and remove.

If you get caught in a “generator overload” and forget — well, that’s one burnt dosa, but still less catastrophic than losing 100 engineers.

⚖️ Step 4: Resource Management (Serve Smartly)

A true leader rations food fairly:

Serve each dosa hot with coconut chutney (or just store-bought sauce if you’re mid-crisis).

If the city’s falling apart, make mini dosas so you can eat between law signings.

Don’t attempt sambar unless your discontent bar is low — that’s end-game content.

🧠 Pro Survival Tips

Automation: Use a non-stick pan = less attention, fewer breakdowns.

Multitask cycles: Each dosa cooks in about 2 minutes — perfect to check your coal pile or reassign workers between flips.

Heat management: Don’t turn off the generator (stove). Dosas hate low heat as much as New Londoners hate cold.

Discontent management: If dosas start sticking, clean the tawa with half an onion dipped in oil. Works like signing a new law.

Morale: A crisp dosa boosts hope. A soggy one boosts discontent. Choose wisely.

🌤️ Step 5: Survive Till Dawn

By the time the storm passes and your city stabilizes at +10°C, you’ll have:

A hot plate of perfectly crisp masala dosas

A functioning colony (hopefully)

A house that smells of victory and fermented rice

And if things go south in Frostpunk, remember: you can always rebuild — but burnt batter is forever lost.

207778019.review:
Many city and empire management games suffer from unimportant decisions in the later sections. As you economy becomes bigger, smaller decision become less important, and I get bored making many small decisions that at most will only affect my efficiency by a few percent.
Frostpunk solves this with escalating difficult and ongoing challenges. Every decision you make will affect the wellbeing of your people, and they let you know. Getting a building done 10% sooner is life and death for some of your citizens, your failures will cost them, and in turn they can become less cooperative and threaten to overthrow you if you work them too hard or neglect their needs.
 You're also stuck with your city the entire game. Make a bad planning choice? that might cost you a house in an inner ring, now your people are homeless and are going to get sick. This makes city planning far more important than an empire game where you can just conquer others.
Highly recommend, I'm stuck to my PC start to finish whenever I do a run.

207385959.review:
Ok , I love strategy games since I was very young. This game at the beginning even with easy mode was very hard to play!!!! , kept dying all the time till I figured out the main elements to build and research to reach the end and finish the last storm. 

That’s how to do it :

❄️ FROSTPUNK REALISTIC SURVIVAL PLAN (A NEW HOME)
________________________________________
🧭 PHASE 1: DAYS 1–4 — ESTABLISH BASIC SURVIVAL
Goal: Get everyone sheltered, feed the city, and turn the Generator on steadily.
Step 1 — Early Day 1: Plan & Assign
•	Pause immediately and check your surroundings.
•	Assign:
o	15 Workers → Coal Pile
o	15 Workers → Wood Crate
o	15 Workers → Steel Wreckage
•	Engineers stay free to build.
Step 2 — Build Priority
•	First builds:
o	5–6 Tents (close to Generator center)
o	1 Medical Post
o	1 Cookhouse
•	Save at least 15 Wood + 5 Steel for a Workshop tomorrow.
Step 3 — Turn on Generator
•	Once Coal reaches 200 → turn Generator ON before 18:00.
•	Keep it on continuously overnight.
Step 4 — Laws
•	📜 Day 1: Sign Emergency Shift → use once on Workshop later.
•	📜 Day 3: Sign Extended Shift → apply to Workshops & resource posts.
Step 5 — Day 2–3 Developments
•	Build your first Workshop → assign Engineers.
•	Begin researching:
1.	Faster Gathering
2.	Heaters
•	Build 1 Gathering Post (once you have ~40–50 Wood).
o	Cover at least 2 piles if possible.
•	Slowly move workers from open piles to the post by Day 3.
Step 6 — Shelter & Food
•	Finish remaining Tents by Day 3–4.
•	Ensure Cookhouse is staffed and food is cooked each evening.
•	Add another Medical Post if sickness spikes.






⚙️ PHASE 2: DAYS 5–8 — BUILD THE FOUNDATION
Goal: Stabilize food and coal, expand research, prepare to scout.
Step 1 — Expand Workshops
•	Build a 2nd Workshop (or 3rd if wood allows).
•	Keep Engineers evenly distributed.
Step 2 — Research Path
1.	Beacon
2.	Sawmill
3.	Coal Thumper
4.	Generator Power Upgrade I
Step 3 — Scouting
•	Once Beacon is researched → build it immediately.
•	Send your first Scout Team to Lost Expedition (rescue survivors).
•	Build a second team when possible.
Step 4 — Law Progression
•	After Extended Shift:
o	Sign Cemetery (avoids discontent from deaths).
o	Then Fighting Arena (boosts hope).
Step 5 — Resource Management
•	Build Sawmill to replace scattered wood crates.
•	Build Coal Thumper near Gathering Posts for long-term coal supply.
•	Maintain at least 2 Gathering Posts by now.
















🔥 PHASE 3: DAYS 9–12 — STABILIZE & SECURE HEAT
Goal: Prepare for colder temperatures and stable economy.
Step 1 — Infrastructure Growth
•	Add Steelworks (steel wrecks will soon run out).
•	Continue to expand your tent ring — everyone must be housed.
•	Keep homes within Generator range.
Step 2 — Heating & Energy
•	Research:
1.	Steam Hub
2.	Heater Upgrade
3.	Generator Range Upgrade I
•	Build 1–2 Steam Hubs for outer buildings (Cookhouse, Gathering Posts).
Step 3 — Law Development
•	Choose a Path:
o	Order (recommended for first playthrough) → start with Neighbourhood Watch
o	or Faith → start with House of Prayer
Step 4 — Scouts & Expansion
•	Keep Scouts active — aim for Winterhome discovery by Day 12.
•	Prioritize resources and rescue sites (don’t chase every ruin yet).





























🏗️ PHASE 4: DAYS 13–18 — EXPANSION & ORGANIZATION
Goal: Automate, manage discontent, and boost production efficiency.
Step 1 — Industry Upgrades
•	Research:
1.	Wall Drill (if wood low)
2.	Coal Mine (more efficient than Thumper)
3.	Generator Efficiency Upgrade I
•	Keep 3–4 Workshops working full-time.
Step 2 — Law Order
•	If Order Path:
o	Patrols → Propaganda Centre → Foreman.
•	If Faith Path:
o	Shrines → Field Kitchens → Temple.
Step 3 — Build Automations
•	Research Automatons → assign first one to Coal Mine or Sawmill.
•	They work nonstop — crucial for later storms.
Step 4 — Population Growth
•	New arrivals come via events — build extra Tents/Bunkhouses for them.
•	Expect some sickness; prepare extra Medical Posts or Care House.

















❄️ PHASE 5: DAYS 19–25 — PREPARE FOR THE FINAL STORM
Goal: Fortify your city, stockpile resources, and heat upgrades.
Step 1 — Resource Stockpiling
•	Build multiple Resource Depots:
o	3 for Coal
o	1 for Food Rations
o	1 for Wood or Steel
•	Store at least:
o	10,000+ Coal
o	200+ Cooked Food
o	1000+ Wood
Step 2 — Housing & Heat
•	Upgrade Tents → Bunkhouses → Houses if possible.
•	Research:
o	Generator Power Upgrade II
o	Generator Range II
o	Advanced Heaters
Step 3 — Laws
•	Finish morale-boosting laws:
o	Propaganda Centre/Temple
o	Foreman or Sermons for productivity.
Step 4 — Final Researches
•	Steam Coal Mine
•	House Insulation
•	Automaton Integration























🌪️ PHASE 6: DAYS 26–END — THE GREAT STORM
Goal: Survive through the -120°C freeze and keep hope alive.
Step 1 — Before the Storm Hits
•	Recall all Scouts; they’ll die if left outside.
•	Shut down non-essential buildings (Sawmills, Steelworks).
•	Keep Cookhouse, Medical, Generator, and Workshops active only.
•	Fill Resource Depots completely.
Step 2 — During the Storm
•	Overdrive strategically — don’t exceed 90% stress.
•	Switch off Heaters in empty buildings.
•	Keep citizens in warm areas only.
•	Distribute food evenly; no starvation allowed.
Step 3 — Managing Hope
•	Constantly trigger:
o	Patrols / Sermons / Propaganda Broadcasts.
•	Keep Discontent below 50%.
•	Heal everyone before temperature minimum hits.
Step 4 — The End
•	Generator stress will climb — use short bursts of Overdrive.
•	If you reach the final dawn without collapse, you’ve survived humanity’s last city. 🌅


























⚖️ RESEARCH PRIORITY SUMMARY (Realistic Path)
1.	Faster Gathering
2.	Heaters
3.	Beacon
4.	Sawmill
5.	Coal Thumper or Mine
6.	Generator Power I
7.	Steam Hub
8.	Heater Upgrade
9.	Generator Range I
10.	Coal Mine / Wall Drill
11.	Generator Efficiency
12.	House Insulation
13.	Power II / Range II
14.	Automatons & Integration

207353841.review: As you'll see, I barely have any hours into the game. I failed my first try, and then did pretty well (and still alive) on my 2nd try on Normal Difficulty. Ultimately this is great game, but it's just not a great game FOR ME. I personally have found that I just can't sit here and frequently pause the game so I can maintain the micromanagement that it requires to keep your game progressing and citizens alive to push forward. This is 100% a daunting game that requires close attention to detail, micromanagement, organization and planning out many steps ahead while knowing that every choice will have a consequence down the road. There's no way I'd pay it's full price $30 price tag, thankfully I got it awhile ago on sale for like $9.99. When FP2 is only liek 15 bucks more at regular price. If you don't care about "The Lore" of the game. I'd jump to the more current and modern title. BUT AGAIN, THIS IS A GREAT GAME if you love survival and/or real time strategy games. It's 100% worth a try, but I'd highly suggest watching youtube videos or reading forums to get a basic understanding and some insanely helpful advice before ever clicking the "start" button. lol

207342593.review:
As a longtime enthusiast of the city builder, survival, and management genres, I can confidently say that Frostpunk instantly hooked me. It's rare for a game to feel both utterly familiar in its core mechanics yet uniquely punishing in its execution, but Frostpunk managed to hit that sweet spot right from the first play session.

This game is not an easy commitment. The initial learning curve is steep; the mechanics around heat, coal, food, and hope/discontent create a brutally interconnected system where one wrong decision can trigger a rapid, irreversible spiral into chaos. I distinctly remember struggling fiercely to keep my citizens from freezing or rioting in the early hours. Completing even the first scenario felt like scaling a glacier.

However, once you move past that initial overwhelming challenge and truly begin to understand the deep, desperate dynamics (how to balance morale with utility, how to make agonizing moral compromises for the greater good) the game transforms. It stops being a series of crises and starts being a masterful exercise in efficient, ethical-adjacent resource optimization.

Frostpunk quickly earned its place as one of the best city-management titles in my library. Be warned: this is not an easy game. But if you love feeling constantly on the edge, where every resource point matters and every Law you pass carries profound weight, you will find an immensely rewarding experience here. You'll finish each session feeling drained, but with the profound satisfaction of having survived another day against impossible odds.

207149194.review:
You will feel as the captain does, weighing the life of a man against a handful of coal. Nothing is ever enough, you are in constant crisis, and you must make sacrifices to survive. The screws are always turning. To some people this isn't appealing, but I love it! It's extremely well designed. 

The game achieves this tightness of design by having scripted story events at fixed intervals. This means it's not really a sandbox city builder, and replayability is low. The trade-off is the game is that much more focused. There are also some events you can't reasonably predict, but if your run fails you can usually trace it back to a skill issue. Even on the highest difficulty, you have a lot of leeway. You'll have a dozen failed attempts learning the game, one or two where you know what you're doing and make a series of falling-domino errors, and one where you will finally succeed. Scenarios are a little replayable with the different playstyles and law systems, but really only a nerd goes back to redo a beaten scenario. 

It's pretty much just a series of math problems. You'll get your money's worth

206887787.review:
Like other reviewers have mentioned, this game has no real replayability.

It is pretty simple, straight-forward and in theory (at least) there are only two outcomes:
Fascist dictatorship
Religious kookery

I rejected both and still "won," but I saw how at the end many of the technological advancements did not matter because a cold storm came through with temperatures so cold no human could realistcally live through anyway.

Regardless...I "beat" it...and I simply have NO desire to replay.

Yes - it was pretty fun the first time through, but meh.

The most important resource to gather at all times is coal...but again, the final storm is so cold many people die NO MATTER HOW MUCH coal the player stockpiles.

Many of the technology tree advancements seemed like a bit of dead end, and really, the player MUST triage and amputate because otherwise there are many, many invalids consuming resources and not contributing.

Finally, there is this lame manufactured drama of a group of settlers who wish to return to London, and the player (again!) MUST prevent them from leaving by moving in the direction of fascism or kookery...when I was thinking the whole time:
"JUST LET THEM LEAVE!"
For real, if these people do not want to stay, and are just stirring up trouble - banish them!

Simple!

No drama needed and then go on about one's day.

In conclusion - there is a wonderful idea here.
I LOVE the bleak world, survival, exploration and resource gathering.
I LOVE the graphics and much (but not all) of the day-to-day gameplay

But really, because of how limited and railroaded the story and game is - there is no reason to replay because there is no innovation beyond a certain point.

205987013.review:
[b]Recommended![/b]

I managed to pick up Frostpunk and its two DLC when it was on sale for a total of £7.37 which feels like a total steal. It's rare that I get this immediately hooked on a game and no-life it to completion, putting in 62.6 hours in the space of 10 days. My friend recommended this to me when I was asking for more resource management games to try, and it did not disappoint!

[b]GAMEPLAY: 4/5[/b]
Balance: Frostpunk does an excellent job at balancing the game - I completed all scenarios on Normal mode, and they were sufficiently challenging that I had to restart several of them multiple times, or look up guides online to try to figure out how to complete them. I felt really satisfied completing the game on Normal, and there's harder difficulties available which is great for fans - I always get frustrated when I enjoy a game but even the hardest difficulty feels a bit too easy.

Onboarding: The quest log is a great feature that really keeps you on track so you know exactly what you need to do in the game, and how long you've got left to do it. The tutorial system was good too - popping up just when I needed it, and always being available in the top left hand corner when I wanted to refer to it. However, there were a few features that weren't explained well that I ended up having to google - things like needing a Gathering Post in order to get Coal from your Coal Thumpers, or understanding if certain buildings needed to be built near workplaces or homes to have an effect.

Variety: The four main scenarios do a great job at varying how you approach the game, which helped keep the game fresh. Very light spoilers on what each mode is about: [spoiler]First one: stockpile resources for an oncoming storm. Second: Micromanage a very small task force and keep 4 buildings warm at all times. Third: Keep a massive population alive. Fourth: Deconstruct and rebuild a poorly designed city.[/spoiler] The DLCs also change up the gameplay a lot, including providing a beautiful and refreshing visual overhaul for The Last Autumn. That said, once I found a "winning formula" of how to get started and what Law/Order paths to take, sometimes the game became a bit stale as I only really needed to minimally change my Law/Order routes and Tech paths for each scenario.

Ease of use: I felt frustrated with a few aspects of this game, like trying to click on a heater that was next to a building and clicking on the wrong thing. Or trying to turn off Extended Shift to lower Discontent, but not knowing what building still had it on, and having to click through all my buildings one-by-one to find it (it's always the Cookhouse I forget about). Or trying to dismiss sick workers from a workplace, but then not being able to choose healthy workers to replace them with and having to add/remove a few times to get the right people. Or, and I know this is user error - but on a few occasions I accidentally pressed the big X for "dismantle" thinking it was going to close the window for the building I was looking at. Also, frustratingly, sometimes buildings will expand to take up more space than they're meant to, which often led to a lot of annoying deleting and redrawing of roads to get them to fit right.

[b]STORY AND CHARACTERS: 4/5[/b]
Frostpunk does a great job at getting you to care about your citizens and the society you're building. There are some real moral consequences for your actions (or inactions) that I felt were moving, such as [spoiler]when I was preparing to evacuate Winterhome, and I hadn't provided any prosthetics for my amputees, and an amputee approached me and said "I understand why you're leaving us behind" - god I felt guilty![/spoiler]. There's usually great hidden consequences for a lot of the choices in the game, like whether you let a citizen break the rules or not, which often leads to branching story arcs that can be resolved in different ways. It really made me weigh up my options in a lot of scenarios - which makes you really consider whether you're going to take a good path VS an evil path. However I can't extend that same praise to the DLC - I found that by the end of The Last Autumn, I just felt frustrated with my workforce rather than protective of them like how I did in other scenarios. Also, there didn't seem to be any consequences for your actions in On The Edge, which made the choices there feel a bit empty.

I liked how some narrative arcs in A New Home, Winterhome and The Last Autumn all tied in to each other - it would have been cool if your actions in each scenario could have led to a different result for the others, but I understand that would have been massively out of scope!

Final note on story - other than [spoiler]Winterhome, where I felt compelled to replay so I could save as many people as I could[/spoiler] - I didn't really feel a lot of motivation to repeat scenarios to try to get a better result. The story is dreary - which is necessary, when you're telling a tale about survival and finding hope in the darkness - but many of the scenarios just feel a bit hopeless and doomed. There were some small story moments like [spoiler]getting a thank you note for setting up care homes or child shelters[/spoiler] that felt uplifting, but overall I didn't really come away from this game with any warm fuzzy feelings about saving the future of mankind, or feeling strongly motivated to replay it all again on a harder difficulty.

[b]MUSIC: 3/5[/b]
It kind of feels like this game just has one music track - it's good, very emotional and tonally right for this game. It also didn't get on my nerves in over 62 hours of gameplay, which is a bonus. However I think for me to rank this a bit more highly I would have liked a few more tracks for variety.

[b]GRAPHICS: 4/5[/b]
Overall the game is very immersive and beautiful, however I often found that it was difficult for me to tell my buildings apart (especially when they were covered in snow) which often led to a lot of madly clicking around trying to see what's what. Sometimes I swapped into the thermal map mode just to be able to see where my tents were, which wasn't really the intended use of that screen. I also found that a lot of the resource piles were a bit hard to see- there's a stack of wood crates in On The Edge that I didn't even notice until nearly the end of the game. I would have liked a simplified outline/strategic view like in Civ6, perhaps.

[b]FINAL SCORE: 4/5[/b]
Frostpunk is an excellent resource management game. I like to describe it to my friends "as if Civ6 and The Long Dark had a baby". It's immersive and great at sucking you in to the story and gameplay, and I'd highly recommend this to any fan of the genre!

205767831.review:
I bought Frostpunk to relax… yeah, that didn’t happen. This game is stressful, intense, and will make you question your life choices — in the best way.

* First 2 weeks felt chill: build stuff, gather resources, not too bad. Then the game was like, “Surprise! Hope is overrated.”

* Resource management becomes a full-on anxiety simulator. Every decision feels like a do-or-die moment.

* Leadership decisions? Forget it. Your people will complain no matter what you do. Pro tip: if it’s your first playthrough, sign the dictatorship law. It removes the annoying hope panel and makes life slightly less miserable.

* I finished the campaign in 19 hours (Steam thinks I played 3… sure, okay), and the feeling of actually surviving? So satisfying.

Bottom line: this is not a cozy, warm, “let’s sip cocoa” kind of game. It’s more like sipping hot lava while trying not to freeze to death. But if you like intense strategy, tough decisions, and secretly enjoy torturing yourself, go for it.

Verdict: Stressy, intense, and strangely addictive. Go for it

This game is incredible, but it's a little hard for me to elaborate why
Frostpunk's a strategic resource manager city builder game that has you constantly trying to keep your people safe and sound but it never feels like you're 100% safe no matter how prepared you think you are.
This game keeps you on your toes always and I think it's actually really fun since it keeps you moving and thinking. Between prioritizing resource harvesting, rescuing people with the beacon or trying to make sure riots don't overthrow the damn city, it's a really tough but also really engaging and fun game. 
On a discount (90% off usually) you'd be a fool not to buy it, but I think it's well worth the full price as well. I'm multiple runs in with my best being day 34/45 and I've already sunk in around 12 hours. I adore this game.

205080896.review:
When I bought Frostpunk, I thought I’d play for two hours, refund it, and go back to YouTube or RimWorld. Instead, I’ve been freezing my digital citizens to death for fun and calling it “strategy.”

I just beat the New Home scenario on my third attempt (translation: two cities are now beautiful frozen graveyards). Every choice felt like Sophie’s Choice, except Sophie is frostbitten, starving, and yelling at me for soup (which I solved with moonshine soup, naturally). It’s tense, stressful, and somehow fun in the same way juggling knives on a unicycle is “fun.”

I honestly didn’t expect to be this engaged by what is basically a simulator for “average Siberian Tuesday.” Yet here I am, hyperfixating on coal math while frantically turning up the generator because my pretend people are cold. Anytime the populace even thought about rebelling, I’d order an execution immediately — and I wasn’t above sending as many workers as possible to the coal mines to keep efficiency up. Many did die, but that is a sacrifice I’m willing to make. Truly immersive.

10/10. Would let half my city die again.

204723703.review: Steampunk meets Ice Age in Frostpunk—a wildly implausible and confusing blend of city-building, survival and scripted strategy. A group of 100 miserable survivors supposedly drags a 100-ton "heat generator" across a frozen wasteland? Along with some bizarre "Steam Cores" which the game never bothers to explain, likely because how absurd it sounds in the first place. In relentless, stormy weather, they launch a hot air balloon as a "beacon"—from a city inexplicably built in some kind of a round pit? And they survive in a frozen wasteland at -40°C temperatures by... hunting? Conveniently, coal, wood, and steel lie scattered around, uncovered by supposedly constant snowfall. The game is a cascade of unbelievable events, weird storytelling and confusing, unintuitive mechanics and visuals. In the end, the gameplay is a constant, tiresome trial and error because it's impossible to formulate any strategy in advance when you are dealing with such an implausible game.

204525625.review:
This game is so good that I come back every year once the temperature drops below freezing. I've beaten the main story at least 3 times already and tackled most of the DLC's (I'm saving The Last Autumn for once it gets cold this fall). It's always one of the highlights of my winters, but the most amazing part? This game still hits every time.

Gameplay wise, this game blends the city sandbox and survival categories better than any other game I've played. The UI, sound design, progression, and mechanics are all polished to a shine. The story, albeit relatively short, is surprisingly replayable and a completely different experience each time depending on the difficulty.

Speaking of, this game is hard. Don't go in swinging and thinking you can beat the game first try on the hardest difficulty, or even just the hard difficulty. You will lose, you will restart, and you will learn something new or learn a better way to optimize every time. This game teaches you like a souls like does: through failure.

The real reason why I recommend this and consider this my favorite game: it makes you FEEL! This game can actually make you feel cold sometimes based on what's going on in the game. Your emotions will be swayed by what I consider to be my favorite video game soundtrack of all time (which can make me cry even on its own). My first playthrough will always be one of my favorite gaming experiences where I was yelling and pleading for my people, my city, to survive. Because no matter what, the city must survive!

204287646.review:
Recommended
✔️ 160+ hours played

One of the most emotionally intense city builders I’ve ever played. Frostpunk isn’t just managing resources, it’s about managing human lives, and the moral weight that comes with it. A brutal, beautiful survival experience that hits hard, especially the first time through.

❄️ Gameplay & Survival

You start with a small group of survivors in a frozen wasteland and one mission: Keep the generator running, or everyone dies....

You’ll gather coal, wood, steel, and food to survive. Build workshops, hothouses, sawmills, and eventually mines. Upgrade tech, fight off frostbite, and make impossible decisions as the temperature keeps dropping.

The survival mechanics are tight and unforgiving. Hope vs. discontent is the core system and balancing them is a constant tug-of-war. Every law you pass has consequences. Every decision can save lives or cost them.

The first time you play? It's intense, immersive, and filled with uncertainty. Every day feels like a victory. Every mistake haunts you.

🔊 Audiovisuals & Atmosphere

Gorgeous visuals paired with one of best soundtracks in a survival game.

The music evolves with events, growing and changing with each scenario. It genuinely enhances the feeling of dread and urgency.

The city itself feels alive. small things like the citizen coughing in the cold or gathering around fire it all adds to the immersion.

And the end game animation showing the time lapse of your city growing and what choices you made? Beautiful.

🧠 Strategy & Replayability

There is replay value. You can try different laws, ideologies, or go for alternative scenario campaigns, but nothing quite matches the first play through....

Once you know what’s coming, you’ll start optimising. You’ll stockpile and survive more efficiently. The game overall becomes easier, and more static. 

Higher difficulties and trying DLC scenarios bring back some tension.

For your first play through please please PLEASE...:

⚠️ Don’t spoil anything. Don’t look up guides. Just play blind. That first run? 100% worth it. TRUST ME. Try to play through as much of the game on your own. 

✅ The Good

✔️ Emotionally impactful survival game play
✔️ Unique moral choices that actually matter
✔️ Great adaptive soundtrack
✔️ Beautiful atmospheric visuals
✔️ One of the best “first play through” experiences in the genre. Lead me to buy the DLC's so I can experience a hint of the first play though magic again.

❌ The Bad

❌ Replay value dips after the first run
❌ Scenarios are solid but don’t match the core campaign's impact
❌ Can feel too scripted once you know the event timeline

🧊 Final Thoughts

Frostpunk is a cold, brutal, unforgettable city builder that sets the bar for emotionally driven survival games. It’s not about power fantasies, it’s about leading people through hell and asking yourself: Was it worth the cost?

If you're into city builders or survival sims and haven’t played this yet: DO NOT SPOIL IT. Just play it blind.

9/10
A survival classic. Brutal. Beautiful. Unforgettable.
(Best on the first run. Grab it on sale if you only plan to play once.)

I thought I was ready for Frostpunk. I’ve played plenty of city-builders before, but nothing prepared me for the relentless cold and despair this game throws at you. Every decision feels like life or death—and usually, I pick the wrong one.

No matter how carefully I plan, something always goes wrong. People freeze, resources run out, and hope drops faster than the temperature. I try to balance heating, food, and laws, but it always spirals out of control. By day 15, my city is usually a graveyard.

The game is beautifully designed and the atmosphere is incredible, but it’s cruel. There’s no “easy win” here—every attempt feels like a desperate gamble. Honestly, I’ve never felt so stressed from a strategy game. But maybe that’s the point: Frostpunk isn’t about winning easily, it’s about fighting against impossible odds and seeing how long you can hold on.

If you want a relaxing city builder, stay away. If you want to be humbled, frustrated, and maybe broken by the cold—this is the game for you.
