201591699.review:
What a merciless setting and equally tough gameplay! Frostpunk combines base-building strategy with survival elements in a grown-up scenario: settled in an alternative timeline of the late 19th century’s Britain, the world is covered in ice due to a permanent climate change, causing temperatures to stay at -20°C and dropping down to -50°C during weather extremes. The player has the task to take care of a small population of the last survivors in the region of London who mainly have steam and automation technology, depicted in a corresponding visual Steampunk style. You’ll have to make numerous hard decisions whether to impose child labour or apprenticeships, corpse disposal or cemeteries, religious zeal or relentless dictatorial force to keep people in line and working towards preserving the town. It’s up to you which path you choose, each decision always entailing both, positive and negative repercussions in various forms as short as well as long-term effects. 

After more than 20 hours gameplay in a mildly completionistic approach, here’s my pros/cons list: 

Pros: 
+ awesome intro
+ amazing post-apocalyptic survival setting
+ great story
+ daunting atmosphere
+ fresh setting overall
+ fitting, good graphics
+ day & night cycle
+ weather changes
+ random events at different times during each playthrough
+ very challenging last section of “New Home”
+ new angle to base building
+ base building and economy mechanics working well
+ complex resource management system
+ constant struggle with scarcity of resources and people in a hostile environment
+ well-designed law system
+ great soundtrack
+ environment and buildings details well done
+ super tough decisions
+ tutorials explaining basics of game mechanics
+ great exploration events and mysterious background lore
+ sound design well done
+ replay motivation high due to different approaches
+ difficulty setting “easy” makes it a bit less frustrating but still challenging
+ timelapse recap of own town after finishing a scenario well done, includes decisions made and effects of them
+ main scenario “New Home” has about 9 hours play time
+ base game comes with 3 additional scenarios: “Refugees”, “The Arks” and “Fall of Winterhome”
+ forced to adjust play style to other scenarios
+ scenario “The Arks” adds about 5 hours play time, adding some puzzle pieces of the main story
+ scenario “Refugees” adds about 4 hours play time, introducing a class conflict to resolve (which is actually close to 19th century UK issues) and dealing with mass migration
+ approaches necessary for “The Arks” and “Refugees” completely opposite
+ scenario “Fall of Winterhome” probably the most challenging (apart from struggling to understand the game at first in “New Home”)
+ “Fall of Winterhome” great background story explanation, with an amazing, dramatic and tragic conclusion, let me contemplate my decisions and necessities to survive
+ probably double the play time necessary for each scenario to get your playthrough to perfection
+ endless mode lets you choose a leisurely play style or tough survival approach for each map

Cons: 
-	Unforgiving for beginners as well as veterans even at medium (=normal) difficulty setting
-	Tutorials stay very basic, giving you only a rough idea
-	Laws are irreversible, so you’re fixed to one track once you signed them
-	The game demands a lot of trail & error concerning the economic system as well as law effects in the beginning, hence leading to high frustration from early on, yet the developers deliberately designed it this way to convey the struggle for survival
-	Requires lots of micromanagement especially during the first days of setting up your base
-	Trail & error starts all over in the other scenarios, basic knowledge will only get you so far due to different starting conditions and new events
-	exploration events become repetitive after finishing the main story “New Home”
-	Soundtrack becomes repetitive quickly

Endless mode:

+ 8 different maps w distinct visual and building limitation characteristics
+ 3 different modes to choose from (Endurance, Builders, Serenity) - tough survivalist mode, relaxed building and something in between
+ additionally, all maps can be played at different difficulties
+ each map and mode add 10-15 hours play time
+ serenity more enjoyable / satisfying base-building experience
+ new randomised events temporarily impeding economy
+ storms at regular intervals test the stability of your economy and population

-	Historic items for archive neglectable addition to lore
-	Steam core limit of around 15
-	Some general achievements of the game are locked for endless mode, would have been nice for completionists to be able to get them nevertheless
-	After a couple hours play time on a well-advanced map, it seems the engine has trouble handling around 15 automatons and a bigger population (500+ people), resulting in lag, framerate drops which can only be solved by restarting the whole game

Conclusion:
Anyone who is familiar with games like “This War of Mine” will know what I mean with game design deliberately geared towards frustration to emphasise the underlying theme of the setting. Frostpunk follows this approach in base building and economy management in a survival setting, although boasting an amazing story with various scenarios and approaches to take. In any case, each approach is sinister as is the conclusion of the story but it’s a nevertheless rewarding game with a dense, daunting atmosphere. If you’re willing to go through some pain and lots of trail and error in the beginning, before you fully grasp all technology trees, economic interdependencies and long-term as well as short-term effects of laws, you’ll enjoy this game. If you’re looking for a more leisurely base builder, you might toss your mouse and keyboard into the corner in an hour or two after failing repeatedly. 

The endless mode in my view is a great addition to the game, making 8 maps and 3 new approaches available. Actually, I’d go so far to recommend playing an endless mode map first to familiarise yourself with all mechanics and get some routine before starting the campaign. This could take off the edge of the somewhat penalising experience. In any case it's great for replayability. 

Rating: 8/10 points

Frostpunk is one of the most atmospheric city builders I’ve played. The cinematics and art style are beautiful, and the constant struggle against the cold sets an unforgettable tone. Managing your villagers feels meaningful, and the moral decisions you make along the way carry real weight — every choice feels like it impacts survival, not just statistics.

The weather challenges keep you on edge, forcing you to plan ahead and adapt when the next cold wave hits. The pre-made scenarios are excellent, offering varied goals and enough difficulty to keep things engaging even if you’re not an expert at colony sims.

I do wish there were more variety in the research tree, as multiple playthroughs tend to feel similar once you’ve unlocked the same core buildings and technologies. That limited progression was the main reason I didn’t dive into the DLC — though if it’s on sale, I’d definitely recommend picking it up for anyone looking to extend the experience.

Frostpunk is challenging, beautiful, and rewarding — a must-play for anyone who enjoys survival city builders, even if its replay value is somewhat limited without additional content.

Frostpunk: A Beautiful Struggle You’ll Love to Hate

Frostpunk is an incredibly captivating game — a masterclass in strategy and atmosphere. But here’s the twist: it’s also relentlessly brutal. Every time I dive in, I find myself torn between admiration and frustration. I love it… and I hate it — sometimes both in the same moment.

That said, if you’re into deep, decision-heavy strategy games that don’t hold your hand and constantly test your moral compass — Frostpunk is an experience you shouldn’t miss. Just don’t expect mercy from the frost. ❄️

200315073.review:
Frostpunk Review (11 Hours Played): A Cold Masterpiece of Morality and Survival

After spending 11 intense hours navigating the frozen world of Frostpunk, it’s clear that this game is not just a city-builder—it’s a psychological test, a crisis management simulator, and a moral tightrope walk through the harshest dystopia you can imagine. What starts as a survival game quickly reveals itself as a deeply layered narrative about leadership, sacrifice, and what humanity becomes when the world freezes over.

❄️ Atmosphere & Setting:
Frostpunk's setting is its strongest and most haunting asset. The world is dying, frozen in a volcanic winter. You are the captain of a group of survivors trying to build the last city on Earth around a massive coal-powered Generator. The cold is omnipresent—an invisible enemy more terrifying than any army. Every drop in temperature feels like a death sentence, and the bleak art direction reinforces this constantly. Snow blankets everything. Warmth is rare. Hope is fragile.

The visual design conveys dread and desperation flawlessly. The frost slowly creeping onto your screen, the huddled figures by the generator, the cries of the sick—these aren’t just details; they’re storytelling tools. The ambient music and grim sound effects further immerse you in the brutal atmosphere. There’s no escapism here—just brutal realism wrapped in steampunk aesthetics.

🏗️ City-Building Mechanics:
At first glance, the core gameplay resembles traditional city-building: gather resources, build infrastructure, manage citizens. But Frostpunk twists the genre into a constant crisis simulator. Resources are not just limited—they are always just barely enough. Coal keeps your generator running, wood and steel build your shelter, food keeps people alive, and all of it is under constant threat of freezing temperatures and storms.

You make choices based not on growth, but on triage. Who gets heat? Who gets food? Who gets medicine? The real gameplay lies in balancing urgent priorities, rationing everything, and making moral compromises to keep your city alive for just one more day.

The layout of your city matters. Heat spreads in rings from the generator, so building outward is risky. Roads, resource depots, workshops—all must be carefully placed, and any mistake might mean mass deaths during the next cold wave.

📜 Laws & Morality:
After a few in-game days, the game introduces its most chilling mechanic: the Book of Laws. This is where Frostpunk transcends traditional games. You’re forced to pass laws to stabilize society—but each one costs you a piece of your morality.

Do you allow child labor to fill your coal shortage? Do you build a Public House to keep workers happy with alcohol, or suppress their dissent with a Propaganda Center? Do you allow organ harvesting from the dead to save the living?

At hour 5 or 6, you’ll be staring at a screen, mouse hovering over an option like Forceful Persuasion, wondering what kind of person you’ve become—and what kind of leader the world needs in a storm.

🔧 Tech & Progression:
The tech tree provides a sense of forward momentum. You’ll need to research better insulation, improved generators, automaton robots, and ways to find new survivors. Research always feels meaningful, and it directly affects survival odds.

What’s interesting is how the tech tree doesn’t represent prosperity, but barely coping. You’re never thriving—just enduring. Even unlocking automatons (giant steam-powered machines that replace workers) feels more like a desperate grasp at sustainability than a technological marvel.

🌨️ Difficulty & Replayability:
Even on normal difficulty, Frostpunk is brutally hard. You’ll lose many times before getting the rhythm of temperature shifts, efficient city layouts, and optimal law choices. There’s a rhythm to the game: each day brings a new challenge or crisis. Weather drops, protests arise, refugees arrive, food runs out, children freeze.

But this difficulty is what makes the game unforgettable. Success isn’t measured in luxury—it’s surviving until the next storm. And when the Great Storm finally hits around hour 9 or 10, the game pulls no punches. You’ll watch citizens freeze to death in your streets, your food stocks vanish overnight, and you’ll realize every decision you made is now being tested in the harshest way possible.

Despite the despair, the game’s variety of scenarios and moral systems makes replayability strong. You can try different law paths—Order vs Faith, Compassion vs Efficiency—or explore the game’s other scenarios like The Arks, Refugees, or The Last Autumn (which adds a prequel setting with even more strategic complexity).

🧠 Emotional & Psychological Impact:
Frostpunk is emotionally draining—in the best way. It doesn’t ask, “Can you build a city?” It asks, “How far will you go to keep your people alive? What will you sacrifice? Will you still be human by the end of it?”

You’ll finish a session and feel a mix of accomplishment and guilt. That’s rare in games.

🌟 Final Verdict:
Rating: 9/10
Frostpunk is a masterclass in combining city-building, strategy, and narrative into a single unforgettable experience. It demands tough choices and delivers cold, brutal honesty. It’s not a game for power fantasies—it’s a game for players willing to ask difficult questions and live with the answers.

After 11 hours, I haven’t just built a city—I’ve lived through a tragedy, made unspeakable decisions, and somehow, still felt hope flicker at the end.

200296096.review:
I like this game but with caveats. 

1) I love that it's absolutely BRUTAL. On any setting no matter what, you'll die. Period. There is no way to win. You're just buying time for the inevitable. 

2) However, what I don't like is that certain things don't work like they are supposed to. I've gotten into google/ChatGPT about it - & it says "These are known bugs, you need to do XYZ, etc." No... I'm not doing all that. Steam hubs are important, & when I build them they need to work. Not just show red on the 1 pixel they are built on. On nearly every game I've played they have never worked & glitch out, but then they will work on some random building the FURTHEST away from the core. Makes no sense. You will never save your homes, but you can save arks with them. 

3) You MUST go exploring to win, but it's hidden so far down in the tech tree that by the time you get to it - it's too late. You NEED so many things that if you choose A, you'll die from B. If you choose B, you'll die from A. You can't do both. You need workers, so you bring more people, but because you went to those spots 1st, you didn't get the ore outpost. Now you're frozen from being so far behind on it. I've never been able to establish more than 1 outpost, even though I know it's mandatory to survive. There simply isn't enough resources to do it, nor time to get them because you have to spend them all constantly, & your people are always sick & can't work to get their jobs done. 

4) When all of this isn't happening, it's a fun game, but once you've played it 3-5 times, you'll see the tech tree is the same always, there is always the same laws to pass in the same order. You can choose ORDER or FAITH. I did one game 1 & the other game 2. Despite doing everything flawlessly in game 2 I actually didn't make it as far. They were "mad" at me because I had tons of supplies & everyone was warm & happy. Discontent was high & I got exiled for playing well. Made no sense. 

All in all I haven't seen the sequel to this game, but I'm hoping they fixed everything that is wrong with this one. The concept is amazing. The playstyle, pacing, graphics, difficulity, etc is perfect. Things just need to work, & make logical sense. If it did that this game would cook & sizzle so hard that you wouldn't feel the cold at all. 

Graphics: Who cares what a game looks like? You're here to play it not look at it. The elements of haunting souls coming out of the snow with steampunk elements are great. I'm guessing it was inspired by Artic expeditions from the early 1900s.

Story: 5/10. After the 1st time through, it's the same every time, but it will well written & has the potential to be a deep world over time.

Gameplay: Smooth, intuitive, even if glitchy. HARD AS HELL to find your buildings once you set them down. Once the town gets big, you'll lose everything & can never find it. There is no search option & that should be MANDATORY. I found myself clicking on everything looking for 1 building in particular that I knew I put facing me (south) & it wasn't there & 15 minutes of looking, I finally found it right where I thought I left it, only slightly off because apparently I turned the map A LITTLE. Shouldn't be that easy to disorient yourself.

Worth: I got it for either free or a couple of bucks, I can't remember. I wouldn't pay more than $3.99 for this, & I damn sure wouldn't pay anymore than $5 for the sequel. I don't care what they have done it to. Full campaign mode. Fixed everything, added different countries, tech trees, etc. It's still the same game. Multiplayer feature where you try to freeze your opponent might be cool, but I doubt they have that. That might make it worth $9,99.

Overall: You'll probably kill 20-30 hours on it, get bored & move along. Maybe come back to it here or there when you want something different, & to see if you can mix up your strategy a bit & get 3-4 days further than before. It's a cute little game, but needs a little something else to really make it pop.

A truly incredible game. No other words for it.

At least, for the main scenario, A New Home. The Arks scenario was also incredible, until I finished preparing for the last storm and the game just... ended. You don't get to actually ride it out like in a New Home. I think it was actually the most anticlimactic thing I have ever experienced, and it's sort of bizarre because I don't think it would have even needed to be that different from the base final crisis. It undermines the main conflict of the scenario because there's no uncertainty - you know from the moment you find out what the optional objective is whether you have enough resources to complete it or not. The tension of "is this actually enough to ride out the storm, do I have enough to spare to help other people" is gone, because the answer to those questions is just some simple math away. Then, succeed or fail, you're told the fate of your city in text instead of actually getting to watch.

It's not a huge deal. Takes the game down from a 10/10 to 9/10, maybe. Just a really weird thing to fumble right at the end.  Still gave me probably my favorite gaming memory from winning A New Home.

TL:DR This game is very goog

God. This game is an experience. Once you know how this game works, it just flows together so nicely, your city just works. The game is hard, yes, but there is just something special about it, it does not feel unfair, it just works. Is this a game for everyone? No, it is not. The game is hard, and it is hard to understand how to play (at least in my experience) without a guide, or watching someone else play. I saw Pravus play through it, his playthrough is 10/10, would recommend. The music: 10/10. Some of the most immersive music i have ever heard. The gameplay: 10/10. Works into my previous points. Balancing 7/10. I will be completely honest, some things in the game are not completely balanced, like automatons! They normally only work at 60% efficency, to make up for the fact that they work day and night, but you can upgrade them with research, to make them better, up to 90%, and there is a series of events that upgrades them to a total of 100% efficency. At that point, they become significantly better than normal workers, and are only held back by the fact that they cost a valuable steam core. Overall, a 10/10 gaming experience, would recommend.

200065306.review:
As of writing this review, I've just finished playing the main scenario "A New Home" after playing the game for 7 straight hours with a few breaks, and let me say how much this experience moved me:

I honestly didn't expect Frostpunk to be this good, I mean, I had high expectations starting out but I was genuinely taken off guard by how much of a special experience it can provide.
Frostpunk is a city builder type game where your main goal is to keep your city well fed, safe, and most importantly, warm. The game starts you off with the basics, gather resources, build some homes, get some food, with the catch being that everyone is dealing with temperatures of AT LEAST -20 Celsius, and every couple of days, it gets even colder.
Since the game gets harder as you go, you need to learn how to manage your resources and what to prioritize so you don't doom your people to an early, icy death. 
There are a lot of things that you can do to evolve the inner workings of your city: You can put the more educated part of your people to work on new structures for you to build, or maybe upgrade the systems you already have. Every couple of hours you can set laws from your [i]Book of Laws™[/i] to change things about the city's workings, like how your people deal with the gravely ill, or if kids should be put in shelters while the adults work for the day instead of laboring at the steel factory, things of the sort that start small but eventually change how the entire city operates. All of those choices can also take away from or add to how your people see you, controversial choices might make them discontent, but might end up saving their lives in the long run. One of the things it does incredibly well is that while there is A LOT to learn, there's also no guess work. You'll have to deal with RNG every now and then, sure, but whenever you make a decision, the game makes it 100% clear on what that decision will change. That lets you keep more control over the city without being worried something random might come out of what you choose.
Also, the game is incredibly smooth and responsive. The menus aren't tricky to navigate, almost everything comes up with the press of a button and you don't need to consider fifty different things when making a decision. As long as you pay attention, you won't get lost in the game's systems.
It also just feels really good to see your city evolve from your actions, to see your people go from struggling with insane temperatures and drinking thin soup to having cozy homes and better food, from working long hours at the coal mines to having it be fully automated with no need for your people to worry. If you help them, they'll leave notes of appreciation, even sometimes coming to thank you personally for your constant efforts and care.

But what makes this game really, truly special is the entire atmosphere surrounding it. It's a constant mix of hope and worry that keeps you glued to it for hours, I literally played for like 5 whole hours the other day and didn't see time pass until it was late in the night and my eyes were half dead (totally worth it, though), it's the type of game that will hold your attention so hard you forget the rest of the world even exists. But it also makes you care, about your people, about your resources, about your fate and theirs. You don't want them to die, you don't want to submit them to insane work hours or force them to eat flavorless slop, but the cold. Is. Merciless. It will not give you the luxury of not making those decisions. Ride or die.
The quality of the music is also something that cannot be overstated, all of the tracks I've heard so far have left me perplexed at how much they perfectly add to the game, especially during the final days of the campaign. The music changes as the game gets harder (and colder), and each "stage" perfectly encapsulates how it would feel to be in a situation like this. After I finished it, I kept thinking how in reality, things weren't as chaotic or hopeless as I thought, but every single element about the game made me feel like it was chaotic and hopeless anyway, like the world was gonna end if I didn't manage to keep that generator at full power for just a little longer. 
And to be honest, I might as well write an entire essay about those last days, because they were amazing. Brutal, but amazing. Everything gets harder, there's no foreseeable end to the cold, the city can barely function, it's a mess. Everything that you've built is threatening to shatter in front of your eyes, but the whole situation keeps you excited and gives you just enough hope for you to you keep pushing on. You've reached this far, can't give up now! Finishing the first scenario gave me a sense of completion and happiness I haven't felt from a game in quite a while. My people were safe, my efforts weren't for nothing, and the extreme cold hadn't beat us this time.

For a few points I'd like to add, I wanna say the game looks incredible, they really nailed the "steampunk but we're freezing to death" style, I love it very much and really wanna see how the other scenarios might change things up. Again, the game is also really responsive and simple to understand (even without tutorials), you can change things in your city with ease, the controls are great and the UI is really tight, basically no clutter anywhere, it's really clean and really good at conveying information.

And this is what I think of the incredible gem that is Frostpunk. A marvel of game design, and a mustplay if you enjoy city builders, even if just a little bit. It's easy to understand, but takes attention and creativity to master, keeps you interested with incredible atmosphere and a soundtrack that will make you feel for every poor soul stuck in that cold, dead pit. Be warned, It [b]will[/b] steal hours upon hours of your life without you even noticing. And don't forget to bring a coat if you're coming. It's freezing out there.

[b]Highly recommended — even if you don’t like city builders.[/b]

I normally don’t enjoy city-building games at all, but Frostpunk completely changed my mind. It’s not just about managing buildings — it’s about making impossible choices and feeling the real weight of your decisions.

198928710.review:
Welcome to Frostpunk, where you're not a leader — you're a glorified parent to 300 adult toddlers freezing their complaints off.

    People are cold. So I build them houses.

    Now they’re hungry. I build hunting huts.

    “It’s still cold!” Fine. I crank the generator and burn all my coal.

    They get sick. Okay. I build medical posts.

    They lose hope. I build a beacon of hope. Literally.

    “We’re scared.” I give speeches. They cheer.

    “We don’t trust you.” I build guard posts. Now they're really scared.

    “You’re too strict!” I automate things. Less humans = more efficiency.

    “Why aren’t there any jobs?!” Because I automated everything, Karen.

Then immigrants arrive. Yay, more mouths. They’re freezing and sick. I fix that too.
Then someone eats a sawdust burrito and suddenly I’m a monster.
Someone dies — I give them a cemetery.
Too many die — I turn it into a snow pile.
Then they revolt because I made kids work. You know what? I worked too. Every single hour. In a blizzard. On 2 hours of sleep.

Eventually, I had two options:
🕊️ Be a benevolent savior and die of frostbite with a smile.
💀 Or become a frosty dictator with guard towers, propaganda centers, and soup rationing.

I chose violence.
The city survived.
Morale? Unclear.
But they were warm, fed, and alive.
You're welcome.

10/10 would oppress for survival again.

198782969.review:
Gameplay Mechanics: 10/10
Narrative: 8/10
Graphics: 8/10
Sound and Music: 9/10
Replayability: 7/10
Innovation: 10/10

Frostpunk is a brutal, atmospheric city-builder that merges survival strategy with moral decision-making. Set in a frozen post-apocalyptic world, it forces you to manage not only resources but also the emotional and ethical well-being of your citizens as they struggle through an endless winter.

Gameplay mechanics are exceptional. Every decision—whether about rationing food, extending work hours, or enacting harsh laws—feels weighty and meaningful. Managing heat, hope, and discontent is a delicate balance, and the pressure never really lets up. Score: 10/10.

Narrative is baked directly into the gameplay. Whether you're trying to build a self-sustaining city or uncover what's happened to the outside world, the story emerges from the situations you’re put in. It's not character-driven, but it's incredibly effective at immersing you in the role of a desperate leader. Score: 8/10.

Graphics are stark but beautiful. The bleak landscape, crumbling buildings, and glowing generator form a haunting visual style that perfectly matches the tone of the game. Score: 8/10.

Sound and music are phenomenal. The soundtrack is somber and sweeping, adding emotional weight to every decision. The ambient sounds of wind, machinery, and protests enhance the immersion. Score: 9/10.

Replayability is solid. Different scenarios, difficulty settings, and law paths offer a fair amount of variation, though the core loop remains the same. Score: 7/10.

Innovation is through the roof. There’s nothing else quite like Frostpunk—a city-builder where survival comes at a moral cost. It redefined the genre by adding emotional storytelling through systems rather than cutscenes. Score: 10/10.

Bottom line: Frostpunk is a masterclass in combining survival mechanics with storytelling. It’s tense, beautiful, and unforgettable—one of the most impactful strategy games in recent years.

198540589.review:
[h1]Frostpunk – 100% Completion Review[/h1]
[i]All achievements earned, every scenario completed on Survivor difficulty, every law passed, every ending unlocked[/i]

[b]Frostpunk is a brutal test of leadership under impossible conditions.[/b]
It is a city builder where survival is not about expansion, but about sacrifice. Every success costs something. Every decision hurts.

You are the captain of the last city on Earth, tasked with keeping the generator running as the world freezes around you. Managing coal, food, and hope becomes a delicate balancing act where mercy and efficiency rarely align. It is not about building big. It is about keeping people alive, even if they hate you for it.

[b]The mechanics are razor sharp.[/b]
Resource chains are unforgiving. Weather systems are relentless. The law tree is where the game reveals its true face. Child labor, public executions, radical faith — nothing is off the table. A 100 percent run forces you to live through every moral extreme, and few games make those choices feel this heavy.

Each scenario adds a twist. The Ark is about preservation. The Refugees flips population into a liability. On the Edge redefines leadership. Survivor mode strips away every safety net. Full completion is not just a challenge. It is an endurance test.

The game is bleak by design, but stunning to look at. Snow whips through steel skeletons. Frost creeps across the UI. The cold is constant, and always watching.

[hr]

[b]Frostpunk is not about winning. It is about lasting.[/b]
Completing it fully means seeing humanity at its most desperate and most determined. It is harsh, but unforgettable.

198052547.review:
Can you survive one more day?
Much has been said about 11-bit studio's smash hit Frost Punk and its much-awaited sequel Frost Punk 2. One of the best survival games of the city-builder genre ever created. Deep in world lore and choices that have vast, deep, and consequential effects on gameplay and outcomes. It is fun and addictive to play, and replay over and over again, just to see if you can keep a few more survivors alive, or make it through without having to become god or a despot. The expansions include multiple scenarios to follow, and one of the finest endless modes I've ever seen (with unique options for difficulty and challenge). If I have any complaints, they are mostly minor gripes with some choices that have options so bad you never use them, and you can only pick one of the two paths for purpose. It’s not a game issue, but it can limit what you can do sometimes. The game is very well polished with almost no bugs or issues (though I have found people can get clipped into the map and die from not going to get treated). I highly recommend this game to anyone looking for a challenge and enjoy testing their limits and morals is a world that asks you simply this: Who you become at the end of the world?

197836976.review:
I bought this game quite a few years ago, gave it a try, and pretty much quit when I got to the brink of failing in the main story. Fast forward to now, and I've replayed every scenario multiple times, with a lot of endless saves built up. 

In Frostpunk, resource management and caring for your population are essential to success. Failure to do so will result in inability to catch the curveballs the game throws at you to stress-test your city. It is a lot of micromanagement for the uninitiated, but once you learn the advantages and disadvantages of certain buildings, technologies, and laws, that's when the game starts becoming a lot easier. Once you learn what each scenario asks of you, it becomes even easier... at least on normal difficulty. I haven't tried the harder difficulties extensively, but maybe someday I will. 

However, I strongly recommend the average player to go into each scenario blind (in particular, A New Home, Fall of Winterhome, and The Last Autumn), because at heart, Frostpunk is best enjoyed for its harrowing and emotional stories, involving players by forcing them to make tough choices, not knowing what the consequences might be. The difficulty serves as a device for players to sympathize with the plight of the people they take care of, applying pressure without making it feel unnatural, and at the end of each run, the game gives you a chance to reflect on what you've chosen. The game is an ode to humanity, of both its NPCs and the player, and for that, it earns its place as one of the best pieces of media I've ever had the pleasure of consuming.

197618130.review:
"No room for strategy. You are forced to build your city EXACTLY how the game intends (which you only learn through trial and error, or reading exact build guides) or you will just lose over and over. There is nothing new or random about your runs, they are identical every time, and unless you knew that something was going to happen, you are doomed because you made a decision that you didn't know is going to screw you over later on. This is not a good game. I'm surprised it has so many positive reviews." (Chuxup)
I totally agree with Chuxup's review (see above), and I want to add some more critics.
1.  Hunting: how did the animals even survive the catastrophe, given the fact that human technology was barely able to save only a very small part of humankind? Or how did enough animals survive to keep on reproducing after the catastrophe? And even if they did, what do they eat to stay alive with all that ice? With all the hunting, how do they maintain a sufficiant population to preserve the species? How do hunters survive for hours and hours in the extreme conditions during the hunting?
2. Where do all the piles of coal come from? Begin 20th century coal was won digging shafts of up to 1 km under the surface.
3. How did the people survive the extreme conditions of the environment during their migration to the generator? Why were those "havens" built so far away from civilisation? 
4. Last but not least, how is it possible that such a little group can maintain a stable population, or worse repopulate the planet? Baby and child death percentages must be huge given the conditions of life! 
.
I"ll stop here, no need to develop further, these examples are a good illustration of the general idea. At the end, I'm glad that I didn't pay the full price for this crap. After all, a game should be challenging yes, but not a punishment or an exercice of auto-whipping.

197580906.review:
I've always had a real joy and (pleasant frustration) in this title, but it's never given me pause to dislike it.
But it def asks of you a certain mood to jump into it, as if you're already having a stress-ridden day, it's not going to play nice.

I like FROSTPUNK, and I appreciate the Dev's for doing such an excellent job in its execution.

And every playthrough always feels unique, even if you end up following a similar meta near the end game, as the tech-tree usually ends up being mostly filled out in it's entirety, leaving little room to experiment, outside of how many of each structure you have in play.

Early playthrough's were hard, and it taught me through trial and error, not through hints or guides. (please try to survive on your own, not off of someone else's tips and tricks)

Seeing that they released a second game, however; It is nothing like it's predecessor, as it plays more like an ANNO title, wherein this has very specific building management, feeling more akin to an RTS/RPG.

But if you like torturing yourself for the fun of it, go for it! :D 

Also, I have some theme'd FrostPunk Art posted on my profile if you'd like to see!

197536734.review:
Frostpunk is a fantastic resource management and planning game. It will punch you in the face, HARD, if you don't put forward thought into decisions you make. Go down the wrong path early on in the game and you'll find yourself struggling to get enough coal generated per day just to keep the lights on. 

Coal input on lock? Good, but guess what you didn't prioritize heating improvements enough and now you can have all the coal in the world but the temperature drops are getting so severe that your current heat output isn't enough even with plenty of enough coal to power the generator.

Coal and heat good? Opps, a storm is hitting and all your food input is disabled until it passes. I hope you stockpiled enough food to last the storm.

It's a razor-thin balancing act where everything has to be prioritized at the same time somehow. I hate it, but I also love it.

Frostpunk delivers one of the most gripping and realistic city-building experiences I’ve ever played. Every decision you make feels heavy, not just because of the resources you manage, but because real human lives are at stake. The moral dilemmas are intense and make you question your own values as a leader.

The graphics are stunning and immersive. Watching your city cling to life amidst the freezing wasteland is both beautiful and haunting. What really hooked me, though, is how addicting the game becomes. I constantly found myself thinking about what should I do next. Especially when the people start protesting or losing hope. It’s a constant push and pull between survival and compassion.

There are also plenty of tough challenges along the way. This game doesn’t go easy on you, and that’s what makes every victory feel earned.

If you're into management, strategy, or simply love games that make you think, this is a 10/10. Highly recommended.

196709268.review:
[h2]tldr; punishing for new players, enjoying once your understand some core mechanics[/h2]

I wanted to play the game because I found the OST to be fantastic.
Fortunately, the game had a discount and you absolutely know why in the first few seconds of launching the game. [b]HEY PLEASE KNOW WE HAVE DLCS, PLEASE TAKE A LOOK AT FROSTPUNK2[/b]
wow wow chill dude, I don't even know how's the base game is, please give me this at the credits screen, or once I have at least played the game.

Anyway, Let's start a game, the menu handheld me, I know where to click, started the intended experience, the intro really gets you in and within the first few seconds of looking around you already know that you have to gather some resources, build some buildings people needs, it's fairly intuitive.

But here's the kicker, after 20 minutes in game, I realized that I did some massive mistakes, half of the population is frostbitten because I did not understand that I could use the gathering post to provide warmth while gathering materials.
There's a helper on the side but it has a massive flaw: [i]I don't know what I should be looking for[/i]. The helper doesn't tell you with a "ah you have fallen into this trap, here's a solution for you". You can just scroll read an- [b]ARRR to hell that I'm going to make a new game and not repeat my mistake[/b].

So my first 3 hours were about repeating the same early game...at least I wasn't repeating my mistake, just making some new ones.

But then I understood how the game works and did the whole a new home in a swoop as I was glued to the game until the victory screen at 3am.

You may want to compare that game with Rimworld, but in Rimworld events are semi-random and scales with your advancement, in FrostPunk all events are tied with the story progression and once you know them, you can play around and even beat the game in the hardest difficulty deathless.

The DLCs are enjoyable, not worth their prices tho, The Last Autumn being the best one of them with real fun mechanics and a difficulty that is very balanced, but yeah... only one scenario.
And there's no mods or scenario creations tools either, it falls a bit flat. 
Endless mode actually has some randomness in events but it doesn't have a deep story.

Speaking of, stories are very good, you can actually choose your endings as there are real choices. 
Be good or tyranic.
Accept your failures or deceive your population and round them up.
The world is harsh, and the city must survive, blame will come later.

196470085.review:
Frostpunk isn’t just a city builder – it’s a punch to the gut wrapped in snow and steel. You’re not just managing coal and soup – you’re managing hope and desperation. Every decision feels heavy. Do you make the kids work? Sign radical laws? Cut rations? There are no “good” choices, only the ones that suck slightly less.

The atmosphere is incredible – bleak, cold, and oppressive in the best way possible. The art direction, the music, the howling wind… it all works together to make you feel the weight of leadership when survival means sacrifice.

Mechanically, it’s tight – managing heat, resources, and people is challenging but satisfying. And when things go well, it feels earned. When they go wrong? Well… hope falls to zero and the people throw your frozen ass out of town.

If you want a chill little builder where you make parks and fountains – go elsewhere. But if you want a beautifully brutal survival sim that makes you question your own morals? Frostpunk delivers the cold hard truth.

196079304.review:
Frostpunk by 11 Bit Studios – An Unforgettable Survival Experience (Overall Rating: 9.4/10)

(9.5/10) Concept and Atmosphere – Intense and Immersive 
Frostpunk excels at creating a bleak, oppressive environment that immediately draws players into a frozen wasteland on the brink of collapse. The game's visual design, coupled with its atmospheric effects, immerses you in a world where every decision feels consequential. The sense of isolation and desperation is palpable, making the experience deeply emotional.

(9/10) Music and Sound Design – Haunting and Effective
The soundtrack complements the game's tone perfectly, with somber, orchestral pieces that evoke feelings of despair and hope. The ambient sounds of the cold wind, creaking ice, and distant howls heighten immersion and make every storm, crack, and quiet moment impactful. It’s a subtle yet powerful layer that enhances the emotional weight of the game.

(9/10) Gameplay Mechanics – Challenging and Thought Provoking
Frostpunk combines city-building with survival mechanics that demand careful planning and foresight. Managing resources, infrastructure, and worker needs while facing unpredictable weather conditions creates a tense, strategic experience. The game keeps you constantly adapting, pushing your management skills and moral compass to the limit.

(9.5/10) Narrative and Moral Dilemmas – Deeply Reflective and Engaging 
One of the standout features is how the game forces you into morally complex decisions. Enacting laws that affect children, workers, and the sick forces you to weigh survival against ethical considerations. The narrative unfolds through these choices, making each playthrough unique and morally challenging, compelling you to reflect on what it truly means to lead.

(9/10) People and Morale – Emotionally Rich and Impactful 
Your citizens feel like real individuals, each with hopes, fears, and stories. Maintaining their morale amidst hardship is a constant challenge, and witnessing their suffering or resilience adds emotional depth. The game does an excellent job of making you feel the weight of your decisions on their lives.

Summary:
Frostpunk stands out as a masterful blend of atmospheric storytelling, challenging gameplay, and moral complexity. It forces players to confront difficult questions about leadership, sacrifice, and morality in a harsh, unforgiving environment. The emotional depth, combined with strategic depth, makes it a game that stays with you long after you finish playing.

Final Overall Rating: 9.4/10
A true masterpiece for fans of survival and story-driven games, Frostpunk is a thought-provoking journey that challenges both your management skills and your moral convictions. It’s a poignant reflection on human resilience in the face of relentless adversity. This is a game that is a must play for anyone seeking a meaningful, intense gaming experience.

195849592.review:
A survival city builder which poses moral questions.

We've all seen Bethesda style morality systems. Save the city, get a house, some money, and good karma. Nuke the city, get a house, some money, and bad karma.

riveting

Now try this. There are more sick people than medical beds. Without treatment people will die. The temperature drops tomorrow, and you don't have the coal you need to keep everyone warm when it does. Cold people get sick more easily which will make the lack of medical facilities even worse. You NEED more labor to collect the resources to build more medical facilities and keep people warm. You could institute child labor and get a few more workers. On the one hand it's child labor, do you really want to cross that line? On the other hand refusing to use child workers means more people could die. Can you live with that? If you had planned better might you have avoided this circumstance entirely? Is there a third option you're not seeing?

If the idea of your planning having real consequence, and your moral choices resulting in more than a change in endgame color filter appeals to you, then you may enjoy this game.

I can say that with proper planning and execution an extreme difficulty, deathless, only "good" laws run is possible for any scenario, and quite the feeling when the plan comes together.

195813453.review:
SPOILERS IF YOU READ THIS!  
Too many "Gotcha's" in the scenarios.  For instance, in the New Home Scenario, at the end storm, no matter what your heating level is, your hot houses can't be used for food.  The "Ground freezes".  This is not in line with how the game goes up to that point.  Until then, as long as you could keep the heating level high enough, everything was fine.  Even when the storm hits, you can keep the heat level up but the scenario decides you are not allowed to use them.  So if you don't have a bunch of food stored up to last for a week or more, then you loose, period.
In the Arc scenario, even if you have plenty of coal production to keep yourself going and are increasing your stockpile daily, if you don't hit the required coal storage amount before the storm hits, then you loose.  It depends on your progress as to when you find out the storm is coming and thus how much time you have to try to make that rather large coal storage amount. Again, this goes against the game play up to that point.
In the Fall of Winterholm scenario, You are asked to keep a certain number of homes in the "livable" heating range. The untold kicker is, you must keep EXISTING Homes in that range as well as any new ones.  This means that if you are trying to rebuild efficently and build more homes in a better arrangement, then dismantle a few of the original homes, After you've met the heating requirement, then POOF!  YOU FAILED to meet the heating requirement!  This is even if you have many many more beds in heated zones than required.  It's because you dismantled an original tent or bunkbed or whatever that was apparently counted as one of the ones for that heated homes requirement.  No new home can substitute.

These are just a few of the gotcha's, there are many more and for me, that totally breaks the game because it's like the rules are changing as you are playing.
Finally, you only have the various few scenarios unless you buy more than the original game.  You don't even get the "endless" scenario / version.  And, the scenarios NEVER change anything.  So once you've played one through, you know all the stuff that is going to be thrown at you and you can prepare in advance of it happening.  Basically becomes boring at that point. 

I think it was a good concept but the gotcha's, simply to try to make the player "Fail" don't make a challenging game, they make a stupid one.  Further, the ridged scenario set up means it's a one and done type of game.

195647097.review:
[h1]TL;DR[/h1]
A pretty great survival-city management game with great aesthetics and storytelling. On a blind playthrough, the game will keep you on your toes and will force you to make difficult choices, but replay value is limited, unless harder difficulty replays or challenging yourself to make your cities better than before is your thing.

[h1]Story[/h1]
In an alternative 1880s timeline, the world was hit by the Great Frost from the south. The entire world was enveloped in never-ending frost and entire countries collapsed overnight. As the world descends into chaos, some people abandon their hometowns and set out on a perilous journey to the north, where the British Empire had secretly constructed Generators - towers capable of tapping into geothermal heat vents with coal-powered pumps to provide sufficient heating for a town to stave off the ever-encroaching cold - near coal-rich deposits. With great sacrifices, some people manage to reach the sites of such generators, only to find them abandoned. Now, they must scavenge for resources, explore the frozen wasteland for other survivors, and supply the Generator with enough coal to keep the cold at bay...

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
Frostpunk is a steampunk-style mix of city management and survival. You have a number of people that you can employ to gather resources (coal for heating, wood and steel for construction and research projects, and food for keeping your people from starving) during their shifts, and then use those resources to build necessary facilities to keep your people alive. If you don't provide enough heating and housing, your people will get sick and overwhelm your healthcare system before dying; if you don't procure enough wood and steel, you won't be able to build all the vital facilities you need and invest in research to upgrade your economy and livelihood as the weather gets colder and colder; meanwhile, without a stable food economy, your people will starve to death. You can't convert one resource to another directly, so you have to strategise how you balance your economy and the needs of your people to keep your town afloat. You can only gain more people from exploring the frostland overworld, so you can't let too many of your people die or you won't have the workforce to keep your economy running. A major problem with the game is that it doesn't allow you to easily assign specific people to a specific task, or easily switch them out (e.g. 1), people on sick leave will not contribute output but will still take up work slots, and you manually have to evict the sick people before you can staff the workplace with healthy workers; e.g. 2) when you send out scouts, the game randomly picks the people from all of your unemployed workers... but it doesn't take into account whether they're sick or not, and if you send a gravel ill person on an expedition, they will die whenever you try to call the scout party back).

As the captain of the town, you can also pass laws, governing how your people live. The laws you can pass range of increasing shift length to secure a larger output of resources at the cost of making people discontent, to solving specific issues (such as measures to deal with the gravely sick so they don't die, or padding your food with additives to make your scarce food reserves last longer, till you secure a more stable food supply), to measures taken to please your populace or deal with their discontent. While some laws are simple and don't have much in the way of negatives, others require making sacrifices (either moral or physical) and may lead you down the rabbit hole towards tyranny. Whichever law you pass, it becomes permanent for the rest of the playthrough and the game will reflect on your choices at the end of the scenario, asking you the question: was it worth it? After all, while the 'cruel' laws make your life much easier, they can usually be avoided with better management. Most scenarios will also provide other moral dilemmas in the form of story events.

There is an overworld in the game, but it mostly boils down to sending scout teams from one location to another to gather what resources - or people - they can find there and bring them back to your city to relieve your economy. Occasionally, you are presented with a choice that may endanger your scouts, and you can set up outposts at some locations to receive regular shipments of a specific resource, but otherwise, there's not much you can do in the overworld. You'll only ever manage one single city per scenario, and your city is where you'll spend most of your time during your playthrough.

[h1]Level Design[/h1]
The game offers both a story-mode in the form of scenarios, and an endless mode. The endless mode comes in three variations (regular, no pre-built generator, and chillax) and lets you build your town however you see fit without any story goals or deadlines, as long as you can keep your people alive. But in turn, there's not much to do after you've got your economy running and your town sustains itself without much issue, since there's little in the way of challenges to keep you entertained in the late game.

The primary focus of the game, however, are the scenarios. In total, there are 6 scenarios: the base game comes with 4, and the DLCs add 2 more scenarios. Although all scenarios employ mostly the same building and survival mechanics, the majority of scenarios come with unique mechanics or handicaps for that specific scenario. For example, "The Arks" only gives you a small population of people, so you need to invest heavily in automatons to actually keep the city running; meanwhile another scenario turns things around by giving you a (horribly) pre-built city and requiring you to deconstruct it strategically, instead of expanding it. Each scenario takes place over a span of 20-45 days and can take anywhere from a few hours (on replays, when you know what to expect) to tens of hours to complete. Each scenario will offer enough challenge to keep you on your toes from early to late game, especially on your first playthrough, when the game will usually catch you off guard with its challenges. On the other hand, since the scenarios are heavily scripted, replay value will be limited, unless you're interested in trying the higher difficulty options for the scenarios to put your management skills to the real test. There is no support for community-created content to expand the game either.

Normal difficulty (and even more so for easy) is pretty forgiving for a first playthrough, so unless you're actively sabotaging yourself, you can usually get through the scenarios whichever way you like without much risk of failing, but it will still keep you on your toes. On the other hand, hard and extreme difficulties will require much more focus on strategy and efficiency to keep your town alive, though even then there's usually enough room for multiple approaches.

[h1]Overview[/h1]
The Good
+Immersive and atmospheric
+Great lore and storytelling
+Well-designed resource and survival mechanics
+Almost all scenarios offer unique mechanics or challenges
+Harder difficulties offer a decent challenge; easier difficulties leave sufficient room for mistakes.
+Nice soundtrack
+Pause and speedup options

The Bad
-Few scenarios
-Endless mode underwhelming
-Limited overworld
-Few law choices. Law-related events very repetitive
-Limited replayability
-No mod support or community-designed scenarios/maps
-Game requires a lot of micromanagement, but makes it needlessly convoluted

195508576.review:
An excellent resource-managment/city-builder game. It is a challenging city-building with a good atmosphere.

The pacing and difficulty was perfect during my first playthrough, which I later learned is because [spoiler]certain actions accelerate the speed of the story.[/spoiler]. The game does an awesome job keeping you on your toes by using the temperature as a mechanic to both create difficulty and to allow for dynamic/evolving gameplay.

The game also does a great job at simultaneously giving the player lots of freedom while still being challenging. I really like that there are many viable choices available, and many possible solutions to the same problems.

The game is also very immersive; the artwork, story, events, and graphics all contribute to a very captivating experience.
The story is engaging, and I enjoyed exploring the world and finding out what has happened ([spoiler]and will happen[/spoiler])

The atmosphere is also nice. I like the small details like the snow accumulation on the colder houses, and the doors opening when workers enter buildings. 

Overall a very good game if you are looking for a challenging city-building game that also has an engaging atmosphere.

195480206.review:
This game has the best vibes I have ever seen in a game. Just the opening intro alone (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RgPQ3pQ7lFQ) and the The City Must Survive OST (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TpaFE6_nDJw) set the vibe perfectly.

The only thing I have to note is that the performance is abysmally inconsistent. Starting the game up, I cannot focus the game, or it will crash. Opening the tutorial page will lag the game for upwards of 20 seconds. Switching menus can make the game stutter. Every time the game lags the music also cuts out, sometimes not coming back until a minute after the lag subsides.
Also the second after posting this review, I started up the game, opened up the tutorials page for a new thing I wanted to learn about, and the game froze for 3 minutes and I couldn't tab out

Из минусов игры я бы отметил только цену (покупал по скидке почти x2 скидке, вот это оправданная ей цена), и, несмотря на дополнительный контент, он мне неинтересен, игра +- на 10 часов. 9/10

195462987.review: This game strikes a perfect balance of attention to detail and simplicity that make a city builder game fun and accessible. World building is astounding. All scenarios tie into each other down to consistent placement of landmarks on the terrain, as well as references to each other that help you put together the timeline of events. It's loaded with emotional and hard decisions that you have to make in order to survive. The soundtrack is defintiely one of the best I've ever heard in a video game. It lacks certain quality of life features and has minor visuals bugs, but ultimately this is definitly a 10/10

195459968.review: This game is a classic. Must buy and play.

195449656.review:
As a longtime strategy game veteran, Frostpunk completely won me over. It’s a refreshing change of pace – far removed from typical PvP or PvE experiences, it offers a deep and atmospheric city-building strategy game that challenges players both morally and tactically. The cold is relentless, resources are scarce, and every decision can significantly impact the fate of the last remnants of humanity. This dynamic adds tremendous replay value – you’ll want to see how different choices lead to different outcomes.

What truly sets Frostpunk apart for me is the variety of scenarios. Each one presents its own unique set of challenges, mood, and strategic approach. Sometimes survival is the key focus, other times it’s hope or faith. If you enjoy demanding strategy games with a dark yet captivating atmosphere, Frostpunk is an absolute must-play.

195335659.review:
An intense survival game that increases in difficulty as refugees continue to pour in.
But the crowded population is often the least of the problems, as the biggest problem is the accompanying drop in temperature how the player heats the entire city.
When there are warm workplaces, houses and enough coal at the same time, the increasing influx of refugees and the lack of food, shelter and heating will be a cycle.
Especially if someone has a ♥♥♥♥♥ in their head when it's over 100 degrees below zero Celsius, you'll be screaming that this game is torturing the player with its refugees.

195287018.review: Frostpunk is an impressive strategy game that is different from the classic city-building games in that it is built on survival, resource management, and difficult decisions that take you in with its atmosphere. Not only building houses, but also dealing with people's morale, hope and struggle for survival really set it apart from other games I've played. The extreme weather conditions, scarce resources and ethical dilemmas kept me thinking. I handled it a bit badly the first couple of times, but then I got used to the game and I'm doing fine now. The story is very powerful, the presentation is successful and every decision is really meaningful. If you like this kind of games, you should definitely try it.

195238292.review: A really well made survival city builder. It's challenging even on the default "medium" difficulty. Your choices are meaningful, they are not black and white, right or wrong, but most often interesting trade offs. The lore and story are really well thought through and give the game a strong character. It's a must play if you like city builders that challenge you and allow you to express your own play style.

195211908.review:
Don't understand the popularity of this. Perhaps the only joy is to watch the whiny bastards die off. And they have stomach to say that they will rebuild on their own? What a jessie!!

Is it purposely made to be this obnoxiously impossible? You always run out of some type of resource, and if you don't - you run out of people. Research doesn't progress - it just stops at level 1, and it requires the few precious resources you were saving to build a 15th medical care facility staff of which is sick, and prior to that you just had to build a bloody road that ate the little wood you had to spare. So you are left there frenetically trying to gather a little bit more all whilst general population you are trying to manage are whingeing and whining their guts off!
Seriously, maybe they should have stayed where they were and just pass away peacefully instead of doing my head in.

And who in their sanity would put coal mine at a Tier 2 research??? By the time you manage to finish tier 1, you will run out of all those small piles available on the map. And just as you do you will be hit with -40C and your generator will shut down.

Tried 3 times and all of them failed miserably. At this time I removed this god awful plague and am shivering at a sheer thought of what chtonical abomination DLCs introduces...and I don't even want to speculate on how fast the game will go downhill with increased difficulty.

Sod this for a game of soldiers!
